title: Investors have regained appetite for Zambia after default, Citi says
summary: Zambia’s mining, energy and agriculture sectors have drawn significant investor interest as the government’s fiscal consolidation gains traction after emerging from a sovereign default, said Citi’s country chief.
content:
Zambia’s mining, energy and agriculture sectors have drawn significant investor interest as the government’s fiscal consolidation gains traction after emerging from a sovereign default, said Citi’s country chief.

“We have noted increased interest, especially when we have seen significant strides being made in terms of bringing stability to the Zambian economy,” said Lowani Chibesakunda, Citi’s chief executive and banking head for Zambia, in an interview with Reuters on Tuesday.

“We have noted key interests in mining, in energy, as well as in agriculture.”

Zambia is rich in copper, positioning it well as demand for the red metal grows, spurred by the transition to clean energy and soaring power needs for AI data centres.

“You cannot talk about the Zambian story without emphasising the importance of mining and seeing how copper prices have remained bullish,” said Chibesakunda.

“The focus that is there now on copper-related products, be it your electric vehicles, et cetera, is really driving a lot of growth in that sector.”

The government secured 97.85% participation in a cash tender offer for its $1.365 billion outstanding notes, launched on May 29, in what the Ministry of Finance described as part of broader efforts to improve debt sustainability and reduce long-term obligations. Citi acted as sole mandated bank to organize, structure, and execute the transaction.

In November, S&P Global upgraded Zambia’s long- and short-term foreign-currency credit rating to CCC+/C from SD (selective default) with a stable outlook, formally removing the country’s default status.

The upgrade followed the government’s forecast of a rosier fiscal picture for 2026, estimating the budget deficit would more than halve and targeting economic growth of more than 6% after years of protracted debt restructuring and a severe drought.

Chibesakunda said she was also seeing new categories of investors entering the market for the first time.

“We have seen increased interest from players coming through from the Middle East … just to give you a sense, the pharmaceutical sector, the technology space.” In mining, “we’ve had players coming into this market that are mining for the first time,” she said.

The broader investment surge is reflected in the data. According to United Nations trade data, Zambia attracted $1.24 billion in foreign direct investment in 2024, the highest inflow since 2015.

title: HH rallies Mwinilunga, cites development, peace as election nears
summary: PRESIDENT Hakainde Hichilema has thanked Mwinilunga residents for their turnout at a meeting in Kaunda Square, saying their support shows a desire to “continue protecting the gains Zambia has made.”
content:
PRESIDENT Hakainde Hichilema has thanked Mwinilunga residents for their turnout at a meeting in Kaunda Square, saying their support shows a desire to “continue protecting the gains Zambia has made.”

In a statement posted, Hichilema said his administration’s focus is “love, care and more deliverables for all.”

“Road infrastructure remains dear to us and we are determined to work on these and many other economic fundamentals,” he said. “So much has been done but there is more to be done.”

Hichilema urged voters to support him in next month’s election, contrasting the current climate with “past times when anyone who wore a red t-shirt was living under fear of attacks from political groupings.”

“Vote Hakainde Hichilema for President. Vote for UPND,” he said, closing with “Ba Mwinilunga! Mung’wa nankashi!” — a Luvale phrase meaning “Mwinilunga people, you are very many/thank you very much.”

(Mwebantu, Wednesday, 1st July, 2026)

PRESIDENT Hakainde Hichilema has called on residents of Chavuma to vote for the UPND in the Aug. 13 election, saying it is the only way to “protect the gains” made on peace, free education, and Constituency Development Funds, CDF.

PRESIDENT Hakainde Hichilema has thanked residents of North-Western Province for a “remarkable demonstration of hope, unity and patriotism” during visits to Mwinilunga and Solwezi.

In a statement, Hichilema said thousands gathered to hear his vision for “love, peace and shared prosperity.” He reflected on 23 years in opposition and credited the province’s “loyalty, resilience and sacrifice” for sustaining the UPND through difficult times.

“From making free education a permanent right, to expanding investment in mining, agriculture, road infrastructure and other key sectors… we are proud of the progress we have made together,” he said. “Yet we remain fully conscious that our work is far from complete.”

Hichilema called North-Western Province a special place, but stressed “ours is one nation, united by a common destiny.” He urged Zambians to support the UPND in the Aug. 13, 2026 election to “protect the progress we have made, complete the work we have begun, and build an even brighter future.”

“Let’s keep pushing Zambia forward,” he said.

(Mwebantu,Thursday, 2nd July, 2026)

THE Organized People’s Party, OPP, says vice-presidential running mate Andyford Banda has stepped down, and the party has notified the Electoral Commission of Zambia, ECZ, for guidance on next legal steps.

In a statement, OPP presidential candidate Dr. Brian Mushimba said the party “fully respects” Banda’s decision and thanked him for his dedication.

“We have officially notified the Electoral Commission of Zambia and requested guidance on the next legal steps. The OPP will fully comply with the law and the directives issued by the Commission,” Mushimba said.

He added that the party “remains united, focused, and deeply committed” to its campaign promises and will update the nation once the ECZ responds.

Banda’s exit marks the first major change to the OPP’s presidential ticket ahead of next month’s election.

(Mwebantu, Wednesday, 1st July, 2026)

title: Kabwata shooting victim dies, case reclassified to murder, police say
summary: A Zambia Army warrant officer who was shot in Kabwata on June 13 has died, and police have reclassified the case to murder.
content:
A Zambia Army warrant officer who was shot in Kabwata on June 13 has died, and police have reclassified the case to murder.

Zambia Police Service Public Relations Officer Godfrey Chilabi said Friday that Warrant Officer Class Two Matimba Habeenzu, 40, died around 00:05 hours on June 26.

Chilabi said Habeenzu had been treated at the University Teaching Hospital and was discharged on June 25. She collapsed at home later that night and was pronounced dead on arrival at Maina Soko Medical Centre. The body has been taken to the mortuary pending a post-mortem examination.

Police said the suspect, Warrant Officer Mwanauta Chansa of the Zambia Army, died earlier after allegedly ingesting pesticide. Investigations are ongoing.

Police extended condolences to Habeenzu’s family, the Zambia Army, and her friends.

(Mwebantu, Friday, 26th June, 2026)

Grade 12 Pupil Ordered to Pay K620 Fine for Lying About Missing Private Parts. Alfred Mwenge pleads guilty to giving false information to police

Your email address will not be published.Required fields are marked*

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

A Grade 12 pupil who caused alarm by claiming his private parts had been stolen after being tapped by a man has been ordered to pay a fine of K620.

Alfred Mwenge, of New Kanyama Township, was sentenced by Lusaka Magistrate Martin Namushi. The court ordered him to pay the fine within two days or serve four months imprisonment for the offence of giving false information to a public officer.

During the hearing yesterday, Alfred initially denied the allegations but later changed his plea to guilty before trial began.

The court heard that he lied to police at Lusaka Central Station despite knowing that his private parts were intact.

His co-accused, Justine Banda, 31, of Garden House, and Benny Kasongo, 21, of New Kanyama Township, maintained they are innocent to the charge.

(Mwebantu, Thursday, 25th June, 2026)

POLICE in Lusaka have detained two female juveniles aged 15 and 16 in connection with the suspected murder of 18-year-old Taonga Phiri of Kamwala South, Lusaka.

Zambia Police Public Relations Officer, Godfrey Chilabi, disclosed that the matter was reported to police on 22nd June 2026 around 20:30 hours by Michael Malambo, 40, who reported the death of his daughter under suspicious circumstances.

He added that preliminary investigations indicate that on 21 June 2026 between 08:00 and 11:00 hours, the 15-year-old juvenile now in custody contacted her mother and informed her that the deceased had collapsed at a residence in Kamwala South.

She also alerted a nearby relative who found the victim lying unconscious and unresponsive. The victim was rushed to the University Teaching Hospital where medical personnel pronounced her dead on arrival.

Investigations so far suggest the deceased may have been assaulted and strangled. It is further alleged that unknown tablets were administered after the incident in an attempt to make the death appear to be suicide.

Following further inquiries, police detained both the 15-year-old and 16-year-old juveniles in connection with the matter.

The body of the deceased has been deposited in the UTH Mortuary pending a post-mortem examination to determine the exact cause of death. The motive has not yet been established and investigations remain ongoing.

Police say they will provide further updates as investigations progress.

(Mwebantu, Wednesday, 24th June, 2026)

content:

His co-accused, Justine Banda, 31, of Garden House, and Benny Kasongo, 21, of New Kanyama Township, maintained they are innocent to the charge.

(Mwebantu, Thursday, 25th June, 2026)

David Kazadi, Mwaka Halwindi, and Co-Accused Plead Not Guilty to Obscene Material Charges. Case adjourned to 24-25 August 2026.

Police Detain Two Female Juveniles Over Suspected Murder of 18-Year-Old in Kamwala South. Post-mortem pending at UTH to determine cause of death.

Your email address will not be published.Required fields are marked*

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

FILM producer and director David Kazadi, model Mwaka Halwindi, and Ruby Muzandu have pleaded not guilty to charges of producing, possessing and circulating obscene material.

The trio appeared before Lusaka Magistrates’ Court today for plea taking before Lusaka Magistrate Kawama Mwamfuli.

Mr Kazadi and Ms Halwindi denied the charges they jointly face.

Ms Muzandu also pleaded not guilty to circulating and possessing the said material.

The State alleges that between January 2023 and June 2025, Ms Muzandu circulated the alleged obscene material through a WhatsApp group known as “Trip to South Africa”.

Mr Kazadi is accused of sharing videos involving Ms Halwindi and other alleged explicit content within the same period.

After the plea, the court adjourned the matter to 24 and 25 August 2026 for commencement of trial.

(Mwebantu, Thursday, 25th June, 2026)

DNA evidence has excluded a Zambia Air Force flight sergeant as the biological father of a child born to his 14-year-old daughter, a court heard.

The 47-year-old officer is facing allegations of incest after his minor daughter became pregnant. He pleaded not guilty.

When the matter came before Lusaka Magistrate Kawama Mwamfuli, State forensic analyst Innocent Makasa testified that DNA tests on samples from the accused, the complainant and the child confirmed the accused is not the biological father.

Makasa, however, told the court DNA results only determine biological relationships and cannot confirm or deny whether sexual intercourse occurred.

“Pregnancy is not a guaranteed outcome of sexual activity, meaning a negative paternity result does not automatically disprove allegations of sexual abuse,” Makasa said.

The prosecution alleges the accused forced himself on the victim despite being her biological father.

In his defence, the accused, who requested the DNA analysis, claimed he was falsely implicated by relatives in his household. He further alleged the complainant was involved in a sexual relationship with her cousin.

Magistrate Mwamfuli set judgment for July 8, 2026. The court heard that while the DNA findings excluded paternity, broader allegations of incest remain for determination.

(Mwebantu, Tuesday, 9th June, 2026)

PROSECUTORS have applied for a case involving musician Edward Chanda to be heard in camera, excluding the media and public.

A moderate cold wave warning is in effect for most of Zambia from Friday, 26th June to Saturday, 27th June 2026, weather officials said.

A strong high-pressure system is expected to bring increasingly windy and cool conditions nationwide, with lower temperatures, dusty conditions, reduced visibility, and hazardous gusty winds.

Cloudy conditions are forecast over Eastern and Lusaka provinces, which are the primary areas of concern.

Authorities advised the general public to keep warm and take extra precautions, especially for vulnerable groups and people with respiratory conditions.

Water transport operators were urged to suspend operations or exercise extreme caution, while farmers were told to protect sensitive crops and secure loose structures and equipment.

(Mwebantu, Thursday, 25th June, 2026)

A 34-year-old man was treated at Levy Mwanawasa University Teaching Hospital this week for a penile fracture, a rare urological emergency requiring urgent surgical intervention, hospital officials said.

According to LMUTH, the injury occurred during sexual activity earlier in the week. Clinical assessment showed a significant penile hematoma with injury to the corpus spongiosum and complete transection of the penile urethra. The patient also reported prior use of energy-boosting drinks.

The patient was taken to theatre where urologists performed urgent surgical repair, including circumcision. The hospital said the procedure was successful and the patient is stable and recovering well.

The surgical team included urologists Dr. Muleya M, Dr. Mufaya, Dr. Lupinda C.K, and Dr. Chungu. Anaesthesia was administered by Ms. Lydia with Sr. Vivian as scrub nurse.

LMUTH said the case highlights risks associated with unregulated substances marketed for sexual enhancement, which may contribute to unsafe practices and serious health complications. The hospital urged the public to seek guidance from qualified healthcare professionals regarding sexual health concerns.

“LMUTH remains committed to delivering timely, specialized, and life-saving surgical care,” the hospital said.

(Mwebantu, Thursday, 25th June, 2026)

FILM producer and director David Kazadi, model Mwaka Halwindi, and Ruby Muzandu have pleaded not guilty to charges of producing, possessing and circulating obscene material.

The trio appeared before Lusaka Magistrates’ Court today for plea taking before Lusaka Magistrate Kawama Mwamfuli.

(Mwebantu, Thursday, 25th June, 2026)

THE Government of the Republic of Zambia has called for renewed global solidarity and sustainable financing to sustain progress in the fight against HIV and AIDS and end the epidemic as a public health threat by 2030.

This is according to a press statement issued by Namatama Njekwa, First Secretary for Press and Public Relations at Permanent Mission of the Republic of Zambia to the United Nations.

Delivering Zambia’s national statement at the 2026 High-Level Meeting on HIV/AIDS on Monday, 23 June 2026, Ministry of Health Permanent Secretary Dr. George Sinyangwe reaffirmed Zambia’s commitment to work with the international community to reduce HIV to levels where it ceases to be a public health threat.

Dr. Sinyangwe highlighted that Zambia has attained UNAIDS’ epidemiological targets of 98-98-97. This reflects 98% of people living with HIV know their status, 98% of those diagnosed are on treatment, and 97% of those on treatment have viral suppression.

Despite the gains, he noted Zambia still faces challenges: rising HIV incidence among young people, emergence of drug resistance, growing burden of non-communicable diseases, and contraction in external financing.

“Failure to renew global commitment to the HIV response could lead to increased drug resistance, premature deaths, reduced life expectancy and loss of productivity,” Dr. Sinyangwe warned. He appealed for global leaders to unite as they did two decades ago to pool funding for HIV.

He also showcased Zambia’s growing capacity in training doctors and nurses, and expressed readiness to collaborate with other nations on health workforce development.

Opening the meeting, UN General Assembly President H.E. Annalena Baerbock said multilateral action has turned HIV from a death sentence into a manageable condition, but progress is threatened by shrinking funding. UN Deputy Secretary-General Amina Mohammed stressed the need to close treatment gaps, protect human rights, and secure sustainable financing. UNAIDS Executive Director Winnie Byanyima warned that declining development assistance and shrinking civic space could undermine efforts to end AIDS.

Zambia’s delegation is led by Dr. Sinyangwe and includes Permanent Representative Dr. Chola Milambo and National AIDS Council Director General Dr. Kebby Musokotwane. The High-Level Meeting is convened every five years since 2001 and reviews progress toward the 2021 Political Declaration on HIV/AIDS while negotiating a new declaration to guide the next five years.

(Mwebantu, Wednesday, 24th June, 2026)

THE Zambian government has described remarks attributed to outgoing United States Ambassador Michael Gonzales as undiplomatic and inconsistent with mutual respect between sovereign nations.

This follows a farewell message delivered by Gonzales during a reception held in his honour on April 30, 2026, at the Ambassador’s official residence in Lusaka.

Last week, Gonzales warned of what he termed “institutionalised and sophisticated corruption” in Zambia, suggesting that the government’s anti-corruption stance was rhetorical rather than effective.

Speaking during a media briefing, Foreign Affairs Minister Mulambo Haimbe said the assertions were “deeply regrettable, undiplomatic, and inconsistent with the principles governing diplomatic relations between sovereign states.”

He added that the statement contravenes the spirit of the Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations, which outlines the expected conduct of diplomats in receiving states.

“Further, and characteristic of the outgoing Ambassador’s approach during his tour of duty, the remarks, which were delivered during a social event, were not communicated to the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and International Cooperation through an official complaint, demarche, or any formal channel. This underscores a lack of respect for the receiving state and established diplomatic processes,” Haimbe said.

Haimbe also emphasised that Zambia’s relationship with the United States is not, and has never been, centred on aid.

He clarified that stalled negotiations on the Health Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) are due to provisions the Zambian government considers unacceptable, particularly those relating to data sharing that may violate citizens’ right to privacy.

He added that some of the matters are currently subject to litigation in Zambian courts and must be respected, noting that certain provisions are viewed as unconscionable from the perspective of the Zambian people.

Haimbe said the government remains committed to engaging in good-faith negotiations with all strategic partners, including the United States, particularly in areas such as critical minerals beneficiation, in a value-adding environment for the mutual benefit of Zambia and its partners.

(Mwebantu, Monday, 4th May, 2026)

Zambia’s mining, energy and agriculture sectors have drawn significant investor interest as the government’s fiscal consolidation gains traction after emerging from a sovereign default, said Citi’s country chief.

“We have noted increased interest, especially when we have seen significant strides being made in terms of bringing stability to the Zambian economy,” said Lowani Chibesakunda, Citi’s chief executive and banking head for Zambia, in an interview with Reuters on Tuesday.

“We have noted key interests in mining, in energy, as well as in agriculture.”

Zambia is rich in copper, positioning it well as demand for the red metal grows, spurred by the transition to clean energy and soaring power needs for AI data centres.

“You cannot talk about the Zambian story without emphasising the importance of mining and seeing how copper prices have remained bullish,” said Chibesakunda.

“The focus that is there now on copper-related products, be it your electric vehicles, et cetera, is really driving a lot of growth in that sector.”

The government secured 97.85% participation in a cash tender offer for its $1.365 billion outstanding notes, launched on May 29, in what the Ministry of Finance described as part of broader efforts to improve debt sustainability and reduce long-term obligations. Citi acted as sole mandated bank to organize, structure, and execute the transaction.

In November, S&P Global upgraded Zambia’s long- and short-term foreign-currency credit rating to CCC+/C from SD (selective default) with a stable outlook, formally removing the country’s default status.

The upgrade followed the government’s forecast of a rosier fiscal picture for 2026, estimating the budget deficit would more than halve and targeting economic growth of more than 6% after years of protracted debt restructuring and a severe drought.

Chibesakunda said she was also seeing new categories of investors entering the market for the first time.

“We have seen increased interest from players coming through from the Middle East … just to give you a sense, the pharmaceutical sector, the technology space.” In mining, “we’ve had players coming into this market that are mining for the first time,” she said.

The broader investment surge is reflected in the data. According to United Nations trade data, Zambia attracted $1.24 billion in foreign direct investment in 2024, the highest inflow since 2015.

title: Mbombo Among 5 Players to Exit Nkana as Contracts Expire
summary: CONGOLESE striker Idris Mbombo has left Nkana Football Club after his contract expired, the Kitwe giants confirmed.
content:
CONGOLESE striker Idris Mbombo has left Nkana Football Club after his contract expired, the Kitwe giants confirmed.

Mbombo is among five players departing Nkana ahead of the new MTN Super League season. Defender Dieugo Apanane and midfielder Joe Wallace Banda have also left following the expiry of their contracts.

Goalkeeper Patrick Chooma and midfielder Francis Zulu have returned to their parent clubs after loan spells at Nkana Stadium ended.

Club media officer Mike Mubanga confirmed the departures in a statement Saturday. “Nkana Football Club confirms that defender Dieugo Apanane, midfielder Joe Wallace Banda and striker Idris Mbombo will leave the club following the expiry of their contracts,” Mubanga said.

“The club also confirms the conclusion of the loan spells of goalkeeper Patrick Chooma and midfielder Francis Zulu, who will return to their parent club,” he added.

Mubanga said Nkana thanked all five players for their service and wished them well. The club said further updates on squad preparations for the new season would be communicated in due course.

(Mwebantu, Tuesday, 16th June, 2026)

British High Commissioner to Zambia Celebrates World Bicycle Day with One YeYe international

Your email address will not be published.Required fields are marked*

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

WORLD Bicycle Day was marked in style Monday with a morning ride led by British High Commissioner to Zambia H.E. Rebecca Terzeon and Zambia’s Cultural Ambassador and “tall bicycle” icon, One YeYe International.

After the ride, the group gathered for breakfast at the British Residence in Lusaka. The conversation centered on One YeYe International’s cycling journeys across Zambia, his recent trip to Cape Town, future plans, and Zambia’s rich cultural heritage.

The moment carried extra meaning as H.E. Terzeon and One YeYe International reflected on past meetings at Zambia’s cultural ceremonies and finally completed a bike ride together. To mark the occasion, the High Commissioner presented One YeYe International with a gift celebrating their shared passion for cultural awareness and collaboration.

“Days like this remind us how diverse, rich, and fun Zambian culture is — and that sometimes, it’s best experienced on two wheels,”

ZAMBIAN engineer, amateur runner and businessman Ben Bwalya completed his 10th Sanlam Cape Town Marathon on Sunday, marking another milestone in his long-distance running career. Bwalya becomes the second Zambian to run 10 Cape Town marathons, after Ronald Mambwe, who has run 12.

The achievement places Bwalya among the event’s most consistent long-distance participants. Bwalya, proprietor of Climate Control Botswana and Climate Control Zambia, is also a Fellow of the Engineering Institution of Zambia and a recipient of an honorary doctorate and the ZABA Businessman of the Year award. He said marathon running remains a key personal passion.

“I am so grateful to my family, friends, and everyone who has been part of this journey. Every kilometre carried your support,” he said.

He said the sport continues to teach him discipline, resilience and consistency — values he also applies in his professional work.

The Sanlam Cape Town Marathon is an official Abbott World Marathon Majors candidate race. It is bidding to become the seventh World Marathon Major and the first in Africa.

(Mwebantu, Sunday, 24th May, 2026)

NORA Häuptle will lead Zambia’s Copper Queens at the Women’s Africa Cup of Nations after the Football Association of Zambia resolved all outstanding matters with the coach, FAZ said on Sunday.

This is according to a statement issued by FAZ head of media and communication Nkweto Tembwe.

FAZ acting deputy general secretary Iva Lengwe said the issues surrounding Häuptle’s future were settled following meetings with FAZ President Keith Mweemba. Häuptle has submitted her travel itinerary and is expected in Zambia ahead of the Four Nations Tournament in the June FIFA window.

“As things stand at present, Coach Nora will certainly lead the Copper Queens at the forthcoming Women’s Africa Cup of Nations tournament,” Lengwe said.

The tournament will feature Zambia, Kenya, Zimbabwe and Burkina Faso as preparation for the WAFCON finals in July. Lengwe said FAZ is focused on ensuring the team is adequately prepared for the continental showpiece and other international assignments.

He thanked the Ministry of Youth, Sport and Arts and the public for their support for the national women’s team.

(Mwebantu, Sunday, 17th May, 2026)

A moderate cold wave warning is in effect for most of Zambia from Friday, 26th June to Saturday, 27th June 2026, weather officials said.

A strong high-pressure system is expected to bring increasingly windy and cool conditions nationwide, with lower temperatures, dusty conditions, reduced visibility, and hazardous gusty winds.

Cloudy conditions are forecast over Eastern and Lusaka provinces, which are the primary areas of concern.

Authorities advised the general public to keep warm and take extra precautions, especially for vulnerable groups and people with respiratory conditions.

Water transport operators were urged to suspend operations or exercise extreme caution, while farmers were told to protect sensitive crops and secure loose structures and equipment.

(Mwebantu, Thursday, 25th June, 2026)

A 34-year-old man was treated at Levy Mwanawasa University Teaching Hospital this week for a penile fracture, a rare urological emergency requiring urgent surgical intervention, hospital officials said.

According to LMUTH, the injury occurred during sexual activity earlier in the week. Clinical assessment showed a significant penile hematoma with injury to the corpus spongiosum and complete transection of the penile urethra. The patient also reported prior use of energy-boosting drinks.

The patient was taken to theatre where urologists performed urgent surgical repair, including circumcision. The hospital said the procedure was successful and the patient is stable and recovering well.

The surgical team included urologists Dr. Muleya M, Dr. Mufaya, Dr. Lupinda C.K, and Dr. Chungu. Anaesthesia was administered by Ms. Lydia with Sr. Vivian as scrub nurse.

LMUTH said the case highlights risks associated with unregulated substances marketed for sexual enhancement, which may contribute to unsafe practices and serious health complications. The hospital urged the public to seek guidance from qualified healthcare professionals regarding sexual health concerns.

“LMUTH remains committed to delivering timely, specialized, and life-saving surgical care,” the hospital said.

(Mwebantu, Thursday, 25th June, 2026)

FILM producer and director David Kazadi, model Mwaka Halwindi, and Ruby Muzandu have pleaded not guilty to charges of producing, possessing and circulating obscene material.

The trio appeared before Lusaka Magistrates’ Court today for plea taking before Lusaka Magistrate Kawama Mwamfuli.

Mr Kazadi and Ms Halwindi denied the charges they jointly face.

Ms Muzandu also pleaded not guilty to circulating and possessing the said material.

The State alleges that between January 2023 and June 2025, Ms Muzandu circulated the alleged obscene material through a WhatsApp group known as “Trip to South Africa”.

Mr Kazadi is accused of sharing videos involving Ms Halwindi and other alleged explicit content within the same period.

After the plea, the court adjourned the matter to 24 and 25 August 2026 for commencement of trial.

(Mwebantu, Thursday, 25th June, 2026)

title: Häuptle to lead Zambia’s Copper Queens at WAFCON
summary: NORA Häuptle will lead Zambia’s Copper Queens at the Women’s Africa Cup of Nations after the Football Association of Zambia resolved all outstanding matters with the coach, FAZ said on Sunday.
content:
NORA Häuptle will lead Zambia’s Copper Queens at the Women’s Africa Cup of Nations after the Football Association of Zambia resolved all outstanding matters with the coach, FAZ said on Sunday.

This is according to a statement issued by FAZ head of media and communication Nkweto Tembwe.

FAZ acting deputy general secretary Iva Lengwe said the issues surrounding Häuptle’s future were settled following meetings with FAZ President Keith Mweemba. Häuptle has submitted her travel itinerary and is expected in Zambia ahead of the Four Nations Tournament in the June FIFA window.

“As things stand at present, Coach Nora will certainly lead the Copper Queens at the forthcoming Women’s Africa Cup of Nations tournament,” Lengwe said.

The tournament will feature Zambia, Kenya, Zimbabwe and Burkina Faso as preparation for the WAFCON finals in July. Lengwe said FAZ is focused on ensuring the team is adequately prepared for the continental showpiece and other international assignments.

He thanked the Ministry of Youth, Sport and Arts and the public for their support for the national women’s team.

title: Referee Beaten to Death at Football Tournament in Chipangali
summary: A 55-year-old referee, Donald Tonga, was allegedly beaten to death by players and supporters during a replayed match at the Andrew Lubusha football tournament yesterday.
content:
A 55-year-old referee, Donald Tonga, was allegedly beaten to death by players and supporters during a replayed match at the Andrew Lubusha football tournament yesterday.

The incident occurred between 12:05 and 13:30 hours at Mchenga Primary School Grounds in Chief Mafuta’s area, during a game between Mikango United and Leopard United.

Eastern Province Police Commanding Officer Robertson Mweemba confirmed the death and identified the victim as Tonga, a resident of Madalistso Farm.

According to Mr Mweemba, the tournament is sponsored by Chipangali MP Andrew Lubusha and features 16 teams competing for a K5,000 prize in a knockout format.

Tensions began during an earlier fixture on May 5, when Tonga awarded a penalty to Leopard United while Mikango United led 2-1. The decision sparked confusion and the match was abandoned. Both teams met on May 6 and agreed to replay the game from the start.

During the first half of the replay, Mikango United players became incensed after Tonga allegedly ignored a foul. They confronted him on the pitch and, joined by supporters, attacked him with fists, kicks, stones, sticks, and a bamboo stick as he tried to escape.

Efforts by some fans to intervene failed. Tonga was rushed to Chamasongo Clinic but was pronounced dead on arrival.

Police have launched investigations. No arrests had been confirmed at the time of reporting.

(Mwebantu, Friday, 8th May, 2026)

Lions kill cow in Mbala village, police warn residents

Former Police Officer sentenced to 45 years in prison for cell rape

A Zambia Army warrant officer who was shot in Kabwata on June 13 has died, and police have reclassified the case to murder.

Zambia Police Service Public Relations Officer Godfrey Chilabi said Friday that Warrant Officer Class Two Matimba Habeenzu, 40, died around 00:05 hours on June 26.

Chilabi said Habeenzu had been treated at the University Teaching Hospital and was discharged on June 25. She collapsed at home later that night and was pronounced dead on arrival at Maina Soko Medical Centre. The body has been taken to the mortuary pending a post-mortem examination.

Police said the suspect, Warrant Officer Mwanauta Chansa of the Zambia Army, died earlier after allegedly ingesting pesticide. Investigations are ongoing.

Police extended condolences to Habeenzu’s family, the Zambia Army, and her friends.

(Mwebantu, Friday, 26th June, 2026)

A Grade 12 pupil who caused alarm by claiming his private parts had been stolen after being tapped by a man has been ordered to pay a fine of K620.

Alfred Mwenge, of New Kanyama Township, was sentenced by Lusaka Magistrate Martin Namushi. The court ordered him to pay the fine within two days or serve four months imprisonment for the offence of giving false information to a public officer.

During the hearing yesterday, Alfred initially denied the allegations but later changed his plea to guilty before trial began.

The court heard that he lied to police at Lusaka Central Station despite knowing that his private parts were intact.

His co-accused, Justine Banda, 31, of Garden House, and Benny Kasongo, 21, of New Kanyama Township, maintained they are innocent to the charge.

(Mwebantu, Thursday, 25th June, 2026)

POLICE in Lusaka have detained two female juveniles aged 15 and 16 in connection with the suspected murder of 18-year-old Taonga Phiri of Kamwala South, Lusaka.

Zambia Police Public Relations Officer, Godfrey Chilabi, disclosed that the matter was reported to police on 22nd June 2026 around 20:30 hours by Michael Malambo, 40, who reported the death of his daughter under suspicious circumstances.

He added that preliminary investigations indicate that on 21 June 2026 between 08:00 and 11:00 hours, the 15-year-old juvenile now in custody contacted her mother and informed her that the deceased had collapsed at a residence in Kamwala South.

She also alerted a nearby relative who found the victim lying unconscious and unresponsive. The victim was rushed to the University Teaching Hospital where medical personnel pronounced her dead on arrival.

Investigations so far suggest the deceased may have been assaulted and strangled. It is further alleged that unknown tablets were administered after the incident in an attempt to make the death appear to be suicide.

Following further inquiries, police detained both the 15-year-old and 16-year-old juveniles in connection with the matter.

The body of the deceased has been deposited in the UTH Mortuary pending a post-mortem examination to determine the exact cause of death. The motive has not yet been established and investigations remain ongoing.

Police say they will provide further updates as investigations progress.

(Mwebantu, Wednesday, 24th June, 2026)

title: Zambia Records 119 Confirmed Measles Cases, Western Province Hardest Hit
summary: ZAMBIA has recorded 119 laboratory-confirmed measles cases, with Western Province as the epicentre, the Ministry of Health said.
content:
ZAMBIA has recorded 119 laboratory-confirmed measles cases, with Western Province as the epicentre, the Ministry of Health said.

LIVEPublic Health Update | Ministry of Health Press Briefing

Your email address will not be published.Required fields are marked*

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

#WATCH #LIVE#Mwebantu is LIVE from the Ministry of Health Global Fund Boardroom in Lusaka, where the Ministry of Health is expected to issue an important public health update during an urgent press briefing.Let us know where in the world you are watching from? #MwebantuLive  #MinistryOfHealth #PublicHealth #BreakingNews #ZambiaGovernment

A 34-year-old man was treated at Levy Mwanawasa University Teaching Hospital this week for a penile fracture, a rare urological emergency requiring urgent surgical intervention, hospital officials said.

According to LMUTH, the injury occurred during sexual activity earlier in the week. Clinical assessment showed a significant penile hematoma with injury to the corpus spongiosum and complete transection of the penile urethra. The patient also reported prior use of energy-boosting drinks.

The patient was taken to theatre where urologists performed urgent surgical repair, including circumcision. The hospital said the procedure was successful and the patient is stable and recovering well.

The surgical team included urologists Dr. Muleya M, Dr. Mufaya, Dr. Lupinda C.K, and Dr. Chungu. Anaesthesia was administered by Ms. Lydia with Sr. Vivian as scrub nurse.

LMUTH said the case highlights risks associated with unregulated substances marketed for sexual enhancement, which may contribute to unsafe practices and serious health complications. The hospital urged the public to seek guidance from qualified healthcare professionals regarding sexual health concerns.

“LMUTH remains committed to delivering timely, specialized, and life-saving surgical care,” the hospital said.

(Mwebantu, Thursday, 25th June, 2026)

THE Government of the Republic of Zambia has called for renewed global solidarity and sustainable financing to sustain progress in the fight against HIV and AIDS and end the epidemic as a public health threat by 2030.

This is according to a press statement issued by Namatama Njekwa, First Secretary for Press and Public Relations at Permanent Mission of the Republic of Zambia to the United Nations.

Delivering Zambia’s national statement at the 2026 High-Level Meeting on HIV/AIDS on Monday, 23 June 2026, Ministry of Health Permanent Secretary Dr. George Sinyangwe reaffirmed Zambia’s commitment to work with the international community to reduce HIV to levels where it ceases to be a public health threat.

Dr. Sinyangwe highlighted that Zambia has attained UNAIDS’ epidemiological targets of 98-98-97. This reflects 98% of people living with HIV know their status, 98% of those diagnosed are on treatment, and 97% of those on treatment have viral suppression.

Despite the gains, he noted Zambia still faces challenges: rising HIV incidence among young people, emergence of drug resistance, growing burden of non-communicable diseases, and contraction in external financing.

“Failure to renew global commitment to the HIV response could lead to increased drug resistance, premature deaths, reduced life expectancy and loss of productivity,” Dr. Sinyangwe warned. He appealed for global leaders to unite as they did two decades ago to pool funding for HIV.

He also showcased Zambia’s growing capacity in training doctors and nurses, and expressed readiness to collaborate with other nations on health workforce development.

Opening the meeting, UN General Assembly President H.E. Annalena Baerbock said multilateral action has turned HIV from a death sentence into a manageable condition, but progress is threatened by shrinking funding. UN Deputy Secretary-General Amina Mohammed stressed the need to close treatment gaps, protect human rights, and secure sustainable financing. UNAIDS Executive Director Winnie Byanyima warned that declining development assistance and shrinking civic space could undermine efforts to end AIDS.

Zambia’s delegation is led by Dr. Sinyangwe and includes Permanent Representative Dr. Chola Milambo and National AIDS Council Director General Dr. Kebby Musokotwane. The High-Level Meeting is convened every five years since 2001 and reviews progress toward the 2021 Political Declaration on HIV/AIDS while negotiating a new declaration to guide the next five years.

(Mwebantu, Wednesday, 24th June, 2026)

title: Our players were slightly timid says Lwandamina
summary: CHIPOLOPOLO coach George Lwandamina has revealed that his players were “slightly timid” facing big names in Zambia’s 5–0 defeat to Argentina.
content:
CHIPOLOPOLO coach George Lwandamina has revealed that his players were “slightly timid” facing big names in Zambia’s 5–0 defeat to Argentina.

On Tuesday night [Argentina time], Zambia were handed a heavy defeat in an international friendly against the world champions.

The team conceded an early goal, going down 1–0 to a Julian Alvarez goal off a Lionel Messi pass in the fourth minute.

Three minutes from the break, Messi benefited from an Alexis Mac Allister pass to double the advantage for the world champions.

The second half proved a disaster for Zambia as Albert Kangwanda gave away a penalty which was expertly taken by 38-year-old Nicolas Otamendi.

The game was put beyond Zambia’s reach in the 68th minute when defender Dominic Chanda turned Messi’s pass into his own goal.

Twenty-one-year-old Valentin Barco made it five in the 90th minute to finish off a Zambian side that only had one shot on goal.

Reflecting on the result, Lwandamina said in an interview that his team was slightly timid.

“Yes, we were slightly timid playing the big names and the chanting of people, but we were trying to come out of the shell and trying to string some passes. We were even threatening, but all in all we were strong, though we made mistakes,” Lwandamina said.

Lwandamina said Argentina benefited from Zambia’s mistakes.

(Mwebantu, Wednesday, 1st April, 2026)

Contract talks sideline Häuptle from Brazil FIFA series

Zambia, Japan strengthen cooperation in youth, sport and creative sectors

CONGOLESE striker Idris Mbombo has left Nkana Football Club after his contract expired, the Kitwe giants confirmed.

Mbombo is among five players departing Nkana ahead of the new MTN Super League season. Defender Dieugo Apanane and midfielder Joe Wallace Banda have also left following the expiry of their contracts.

Goalkeeper Patrick Chooma and midfielder Francis Zulu have returned to their parent clubs after loan spells at Nkana Stadium ended.

Club media officer Mike Mubanga confirmed the departures in a statement Saturday. “Nkana Football Club confirms that defender Dieugo Apanane, midfielder Joe Wallace Banda and striker Idris Mbombo will leave the club following the expiry of their contracts,” Mubanga said.

“The club also confirms the conclusion of the loan spells of goalkeeper Patrick Chooma and midfielder Francis Zulu, who will return to their parent club,” he added.

Mubanga said Nkana thanked all five players for their service and wished them well. The club said further updates on squad preparations for the new season would be communicated in due course.

(Mwebantu, Tuesday, 16th June, 2026)

WORLD Bicycle Day was marked in style Monday with a morning ride led by British High Commissioner to Zambia H.E. Rebecca Terzeon and Zambia’s Cultural Ambassador and “tall bicycle” icon, One YeYe International.

After the ride, the group gathered for breakfast at the British Residence in Lusaka. The conversation centered on One YeYe International’s cycling journeys across Zambia, his recent trip to Cape Town, future plans, and Zambia’s rich cultural heritage.

The moment carried extra meaning as H.E. Terzeon and One YeYe International reflected on past meetings at Zambia’s cultural ceremonies and finally completed a bike ride together. To mark the occasion, the High Commissioner presented One YeYe International with a gift celebrating their shared passion for cultural awareness and collaboration.

“Days like this remind us how diverse, rich, and fun Zambian culture is — and that sometimes, it’s best experienced on two wheels,”

ZAMBIAN engineer, amateur runner and businessman Ben Bwalya completed his 10th Sanlam Cape Town Marathon on Sunday, marking another milestone in his long-distance running career. Bwalya becomes the second Zambian to run 10 Cape Town marathons, after Ronald Mambwe, who has run 12.

The achievement places Bwalya among the event’s most consistent long-distance participants. Bwalya, proprietor of Climate Control Botswana and Climate Control Zambia, is also a Fellow of the Engineering Institution of Zambia and a recipient of an honorary doctorate and the ZABA Businessman of the Year award. He said marathon running remains a key personal passion.

“I am so grateful to my family, friends, and everyone who has been part of this journey. Every kilometre carried your support,” he said.

He said the sport continues to teach him discipline, resilience and consistency — values he also applies in his professional work.

The Sanlam Cape Town Marathon is an official Abbott World Marathon Majors candidate race. It is bidding to become the seventh World Marathon Major and the first in Africa.

(Mwebantu, Sunday, 24th May, 2026)

title: PACRA registers 9,163 companies between January and May 2026
summary: The Patents and Companies Registration Agency (PACRA) has disclosed that it registered 9,163 companies between January and May 2026, signaling increased investor confidence in the country.
content:
The Patents and Companies Registration Agency (PACRA) has disclosed that it registered 9,163 companies between January and May 2026, signaling increased investor confidence in the country.

Speaking during a media briefing at the ongoing Zambia International Trade Fair, PACRA Chief Executive Officer, Benson Mpalo, said the figure represents a seven percent increase from the 8,551 companies incorporated during the corresponding period in 2025.

Mr Mpalo said the agency remains encouraged by the continued uptake of business registration services and is implementing interventions aimed at promoting business formalisation, particularly among Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs).

He added that the rise in company registrations was largely driven by growth in key sectors of the economy adding that the transportation and storage sector recorded the highest growth at 17 percent, supported by expansion in logistics, freight, courier, warehousing and transport services.

He has encouraged entrepreneurs, innovators and creators operating informally to take advantage of PACRA’s accessible registration services and intellectual property systems to safeguard their businesses and position themselves for long-term growth.

Mr Mpalo said PACRA will continue implementing reforms aimed at simplifying business registration and intellectual property administration through digital transformation, enhanced stakeholder engagement and improved service delivery.

title: Zambians adhere to environmental regulations when abroad
summary: United Party for National Development (UPND) aspiring Lusaka Mayoral candidate Chitambala Mwewa has called for a change in mindset among residents if the capital city is to attain high standards of environmental cleanliness.
content:
United Party for National Development (UPND) aspiring Lusaka Mayoral candidate Chitambala Mwewa has called for a change in mindset among residents if the capital city is to attain high standards of environmental cleanliness.

Mr Mwewa says maintaining a clean city requires collective responsibility, sustained public sensitisation and strict enforcement of waste management regulations.

He said this when he featured on Radio Phoenix’s Let the People Talk programme monitored by the media.

Mr Mwewa observed that the Central Business District (CBD) has become dirtier than in previous years due to indiscriminate littering, vandalism of public infrastructure, traffic congestion as well as smoking in public places.

He attributed the situation to inadequate public awareness on proper waste disposal and environmental stewardship, particularly in communities.

Mr Mwewa noted that the Lusaka Integrated Solid Waste Management Company has the mandate to collect waste from subscribed households and businesses, stressing that waste collection is a paid service and not a free public utility.

He said strategic placement of litter bins across the city, coupled with improved waste collection services, would significantly enhance cleanliness.

The aspiring mayor, however, emphasised that public sensitisation remains the missing link in achieving lasting behavioural change.

Mr Mwewa said the government, the Lusaka City Council and other stakeholders should undertake sustained awareness campaigns through television, radio, newspapers, social media and other communication platforms to educate residents on proper waste disposal.

He explained that consistent public education would help instil a culture of environmental responsibility similar to that observed in countries with high standards of cleanliness.

“Many Zambians adhere to environmental regulations when abroad because of strict enforcement and penalties for littering, but fail to observe the same standards at home,” he said.

He said the country should strengthen enforcement of existing by-laws while intensifying civic education to promote responsible behaviour among residents.

Mwewa is right.  However, the abroad educates its citizenry from childbirth and has laws in place.Every single person loves to be in clean surroundings.  A person intentionally littering the same is beyond comprehension.  Zambians, muli bafiko!!

title: President Hichilema urges Kaoma residents to vote for UPND
summary: President Hakainde Hichilema has called on the people of Kaoma  District in Western Province to vote for the United Party for National Development (UPND) in the August 13 General Election, saying the party has honoured its development promises and is committed to doing even more when re-elected.
content:
President Hakainde Hichilema has called on the people of Kaoma  District in Western Province to vote for the United Party for National Development (UPND) in the August 13 General Election, saying the party has honoured its development promises and is committed to doing even more when re-elected.

President Hakainde Hichilema has praised the   strides his administration has achieved in debt restructuring, free education, and public sector hiring, further promising to reduce the cost of living and uplift livelihoods.

Addressing a campaign rally in Kaoma  District, President Hichilema said the UPND government has demonstrated its commitment to equitable development by implementing policies that have benefited all parts of the country, regardless of political affiliation or geographical location.

President Hichilema reminded the people of how it was in the past for all those who supported the UPND as they did not have freedom to even wear a regalia  for fear of being attacked, with some killed.

President Hichilema noted that the country has since gained freedom after the UPND formed government In 2021.

“Apart from restoration of the rule of law and order, our administration has created employment, ensured that education is free for children, improved the welfare of vulnerable households through social cash transfer and enhanced the Constituency development Fund to ensure all parts of the country receive a fair share of development.

Speaking at the same event, UPND Alliance Chairperson, Charles Milupi said there is something good happening about the road infrastructure in Kaoma District.

He said the Zambian government under the leadership of President Hichilema views road infrastructure as a crucial pillar for both domestic and regional integration.

“The administration’s focus on rehabilitating, expanding, and constructing roads targets several strategic objectives”, he said.

Meanwhile, UPND Deputy Secretary General Gertrude Imenda said people alleging that expansion of Mongu Airport is a bad idea are liars.

title: Hichilema Pledges Mining-Led Jobs for North-West as He Warns Against Illegal Mining
summary: President Hakainde Hichilema has pledged to transform North-Western Province into a major economic hub powered by its vast mineral resources, promising new mines, jobs and infrastructure while warning that illegal mining is becoming a growing security threat capable of fuelling armed militias if ...
content:
President Hakainde Hichilema has pledged to transform North-Western Province into a major economic hub powered by its vast mineral resources, promising new mines, jobs and infrastructure while warning that illegal mining is becoming a growing security threat capable of fuelling armed militias if left unchecked.

Campaigning across Chavuma, Zambezi, Kabompo and Kalumbila on Thursday, the President said his administration intends to unlock the province’s mineral potential to create employment, particularly for young people. He said Chavuma alone sits on significant mineral deposits that government wants to develop, adding that the province would also benefit from new hospitals, clinics, roads and a university if his administration secures another mandate.

The President urged voters to back continuity, pointing to free education, increased Constituency Development Fund allocations, debt restructuring and the recruitment of teachers, health workers and security personnel as evidence of his government’s record. He appealed to supporters to reject politicians from previous administrations, arguing that Zambia was beginning to recover and should not reverse its progress.

Alongside the economic message, President Hichilema delivered a strong warning on illegal mining during a meeting with the North-Western Province Council of Chiefs in Solwezi. He said unregulated mining activities risk creating conditions for the spread of firearms, organised criminal groups and armed militias in the province if decisive action is not taken.

The President cited Kikonge as an example, saying illegal mining activities there had attracted armed foreign nationals, forcing government to deploy the military to restore order. He disclosed that about 3,000 foreigners carrying military-grade weapons had been removed from the area and said some had since relocated to Kasempa District. Government, he said, would continue pursuing those involved to ensure North-Western Province does not become militarised.

Traditional leaders used the meeting to raise their own concerns over the management of the mining sector. North-Western Province Council of Chiefs chairperson Chief Mumena called for a review of the Mines and Minerals Act of 2015, arguing that exploration licences continue to be issued from Lusaka without adequate consultation with local communities that understand the land and its social dynamics.

Chief Mumena also renewed calls for the provincial capital to be relocated from Solwezi to Mufumbwe, citing increasing congestion, and proposed dividing North-Western Province into two administrative regions to improve service delivery as the population continues to grow.

Responding to the proposal, President Hichilema said government was prepared to consider relocating the provincial capital once traditional leaders reached consensus on a preferred location, noting that differing views among local stakeholders had delayed progress on the matter.

Away from the President’s tour, differing political sentiment also emerged from Eastern Province. Village headman Nyamungu of Kapoche Constituency in Sinda District, Phackson Njobvu, said residents in his area favoured political change, citing concerns over fertiliser prices and electricity supply rather than support for any particular opposition leader.

President Hichilema addressed such criticism during his campaign meetings by urging voters to compare present-day freedoms with life before 2021. He said citizens were now free to move about and express themselves without intimidation, before encouraging supporters to mobilise additional voters ahead of polling day, stressing that every vote would count in the August 13 general election.

He did not do it in 5 years. Then he can do it now? Just how? This man needs to be sat down and advised, he cant continue saying things he cant do. You people blindly supporting him, do you now have K50 mealie meal, K12 fuel, K250 fertiliser. Loadshedding took 4 years. This cant continue

Thank you  He is going going gone

Give the man some credit. Is there NOTHING good he has done? Come on now….?

Somebody in the bus yesterday said these things below on the team accompanying HH– Peter Chanda, thrown out of his party– Charles Chanda, bankrupt– Miles Sampa, unreliable and an indisputable loose canon– Mevers Numba – not in jail, on appeal, out of the Canada case where he was convicted, nobody seems to realise this– Mutati, Job seeking– Hamududu, more to do with origins– Milupi, business partner– Chabinga, confusionist

We are voting for Mundubile this time.Thank you for your time in office Sir. Thanks for the attempt

Goodbye Sir it was nice knowing you but its over

Today really enjoying the comments

This a very panic mode, more promises, people have seen it for the past Five years, it’s all rhetoric as usual

title: ECZ Dismisses Claims of Secret Extra Ballot Papers
summary: The Electoral Commission of Zambia (ECZ) has moved to publicly rebut claims that it is printing extra presidential ballot papers or concealing security features embedded in them, as printing of ballots for the August 13 general election nears completion in Dubai.
content:
The Electoral Commission of Zambia (ECZ) has moved to publicly rebut claims that it is printing extra presidential ballot papers or concealing security features embedded in them, as printing of ballots for the August 13 general election nears completion in Dubai.

ECZ vice-chairperson Major General Vincent Mukanda (Rtd) said approximately 4.7 million presidential ballot papers had been printed as of Wednesday, with the process of serial numbering and stitching the ballots into booklets already underway.

Gen Mukanda said the Commission was compelled to address the allegations after concerns emerged among observers, warning that such claims had the potential to undermine public confidence in the electoral process.

Briefing stakeholders observing the printing exercise at Dubai-based Al Ghurair Printing and Publishing, which was engaged to produce the ballots, Gen Mukanda said the printing process was progressing well and remained on schedule.

Tonse Alliance observers in Dubai, including Professor Nkanda Luo, Enock Tonga and Kasonde Mwenda, had questioned the printing of 9.1 million presidential ballot papers against 8.7 million registered voters. They also raised concerns over the Commission’s refusal to disclose the security features embedded in the ballot papers.

Responding to those concerns, Gen Mukanda explained that ballot papers are produced in serialised booklets of 50, a packaging method the Commission has consistently used in every general election and by-election since 2006.

He said because polling stations have varying numbers of registered voters, it is impossible to produce ballot booklets that exactly correspond with the number of voters assigned to each polling station.

Using an example, he explained that a polling station with 502 registered voters would receive 11 booklets containing a total of 550 ballot papers because ballot papers are bound in booklets of 50. The remaining 48 unused ballot papers, he said, are fully accounted for and reconciled during and after polling.

Gen Mukanda stressed that there would be no separate packages of so-called “extra” ballot papers, explaining that every ballot paper would be pre-allocated and packaged for a specific polling station.

He further assured stakeholders that all ballot papers for the August 13 general election would be fully accounted for and that the Commission was neither printing undisclosed ballot papers nor concealing information capable of compromising the integrity of the electoral process.

The ECZ vice-chairperson said accredited political party agents and election observers were monitoring the printing exercise, while the Commission had established strict accountability measures throughout the production process.

These include unique serial numbers on every ballot paper and booklet, multi-stage verification from production through delivery, and reconciliation of all issued, used, spoiled and unused ballot papers after the close of polling.

On concerns surrounding ballot security features, Gen Mukanda said the Commission had incorporated robust safeguards designed to prevent unauthorised printing, copying and counterfeiting.

He, however, maintained that the Commission would not disclose those security features, explaining that revealing them would defeat their intended purpose.

Gen Mukanda also dismissed media reports suggesting that pre-marked ballot papers were being printed in another country, describing the ongoing printing exercise as legitimate, transparent and trustworthy.

He said the Commission would continue providing regular updates on the printing exercise until its completion later this month.

According to Gen Mukanda, serialisation of presidential ballot papers has already commenced, while binding and packing of the ballots is expected to begin next Tuesday. Verification of National Assembly template ballots is also underway ahead of their printing.

UPND chairperson for health Bob Sakahilu, who is among stakeholders observing the printing process, urged political leaders to channel their concerns through electoral reform processes and propose constructive changes capable of strengthening future elections instead of advancing unsubstantiated allegations.

Al Ghurair Printing and Publishing general manager Lakshmanan Ganapathy confirmed that serialisation of the presidential ballot papers had begun. He said final printing was expected to conclude this week before binding and packing commenced next Tuesday.

The clarification comes as the Electoral Commission separately warned that it would suspend any candidate or political party found responsible for political violence following the recent campaign-related deaths in Kaputa and Mazabuka districts, signalling a broader effort by the Commission to reinforce both the integrity and security of the electoral process with six weeks remaining before polling day.

title: Samakai Quits UPND, Cites Bill 7 Fallout and Years of Frustration
summary: Former UPND Member of Parliament for Mwinilunga, Newton Samakayi, has resigned from the ruling party, saying years of frustration finally boiled over after what he describes as persecution following his vote against Bill 7.
content:
Former UPND Member of Parliament for Mwinilunga, Newton Samakayi, has resigned from the ruling party, saying years of frustration finally boiled over after what he describes as persecution following his vote against Bill 7.

In a resignation letter dated June 30, Samakayi told the party leadership that he could no longer stay in an organisation where he felt unappreciated. He was clear that this was not a snap decision, as his dissatisfaction had been building for a long time at constituency, district and national levels.

Samakayi joined the UPND on May 13, 2013, having crossed from the Movement for Multi-Party Democracy. He threw himself into strengthening the party during its years in opposition, and by his own account, the results were significant. UPND support in Mwinilunga grew from around 60 percent to 95 percent under his watch, across five parliamentary by-elections and an election petition. He says much of the financial burden during those campaigns was carried by his own family rather than the party.

It is that personal investment that made the fallout over Bill 7 particularly hard to accept. After voting against the proposed constitutional amendments, Samakayi says he and others were stripped of their party responsibilities.

“The last event over the irreconcilable Bill 7 voting outcome is the case in point, where we were treated like common criminals, stripped off of our portfolios,” he wrote.

“Having organised all these occurrences, I have concluded that I have no value left in this place. The best is for me to leave.”

Before resigning, Samakayi said he attempted to raise his concerns through internal party channels, hoping disagreements would be handled with greater respect for senior members. When that did not happen, he felt he had no choice but to go.

His departure comes at a sensitive moment. President Hakainde Hichilema is actively campaigning for re-election across North-Western Province, and just a fortnight ago told supporters he would not work with legislators who voted against Bill 7. He specifically named Samakayi alongside Elias Mubanga, Gary Nkombo and Elijah Muchima.

Samakayi is now the first of those four to formally leave the UPND.

While the Bill 7 vote was the breaking point, his resignation letter frames it as the culmination of nearly a decade of unresolved grievances rather than a reaction to a single incident. He built the party’s presence in Mwinilunga from the ground up, financed campaigns from his family’s own resources, and endured repeated electoral battles, with the expectation that loyalty and hard work would be respected. The response to his position on Bill 7 convinced him otherwise.

Attention now turns to the three other legislators named by the President, namely Mubanga, Nkombo and Muchima, none of whom has announced a similar move. Whether any will follow suit remains uncertain as campaigning intensifies across the country.

For now, Samakayi’s resignation stands as the most visible consequence of the internal divisions Bill 7 has exposed within the UPND, bringing to an end a political relationship he says helped transform Mwinilunga into one of the party’s strongest constituencies.

title: International Airports, Reserves, and Broken Priorities
summary: It is election time. One politician promises international airports in every province; another questions the wisdom of maintaining large national reserves. In the end, Zambians are left asking; what is going on?
content:
It is election time. One politician promises international airports in every province; another questions the wisdom of maintaining large national reserves. In the end, Zambians are left asking; what is going on?

The UPND was quick to seize on Brian Mundubule’s remarks about reserves. “Why boast about reserves when workers go unpaid and people are starving?” President Hichilema dismissed such reasoning as ignorance, arguing that reserves exist for emergencies. But critics counter that hunger is an emergency too, and so is failing to pay workers and farmers on time. It is like a parent starving children while keeping huge savings in the bank.

No one disputes that reserves are important to a nation’s creditworthiness. The larger the reserves, the more confidence lenders have. But the moral question remains: why borrow $250 million at an interest rate that could cost more than $1 billion over 25 years when reserves could be used instead? No wonder critics now argue that the reported $6.7 billion in reserves is being used more as an election talking point than as proof of the country’s fiscal strength.

President Hichilema and his administration have been accused of overpromising and overclaiming, even taking credit for projects Zambians know were completed under former President Edgar Lungu.

This very instinct is driving the debate over reserves and new international airports. Hichilema once dismissed Lungu’s airport expansion as unnecessary, saying air traffic was too low to justify such expensive infrastructure. Yet five years later, he is promising international airports in every province. It sounds good. But serious journalists should be asking hard questions–how much does it cost to build one international airport? How many billions of dollars would such a project require? And where will the money come from to construct seven of them? Instead of simply repeating political promises, journalists should interrogate them and expose what is realistic and what is empty rhetoric.

The bigger issue, however, is priorities. Zambia needs seriousness in how it debates national development. Most Zambians will never board a plane to travel abroad, even in the next 40 years. Our transport backbone is roads, and we do not even have a functioning rail system worth speaking of. So why build for the skies before fixing the ground beneath us?

The debate should instead focus on improving road networks in compounds and across the nation so they serve ordinary citizens in a meaningful way. The opposition should not just mock the president’s promise; it should challenge the very priorities behind it. Zambia needs better roads — people are dying on bad roads. Zambia needs a new national railway system to ease congestion and move heavy goods. These are the emergencies politicians should be addressing.

Then there is sanitation. Much of Zambia still lacks sewer systems. Across Lusaka, mansions are being built without centralized sewage or reliable drinking water–it is each man for himself and God for us all. The same is true across the country, where septic tanks remain the norm and water sources are being polluted. When will we have a serious national conversation about sanitation and centralized water systems for ordinary Zambians?

In truth, this debate reveals how politicians view citizens–we are perceived as fools. To promise international airports instead of roads, working toilets and clean water is insulting. Zambia must revisit its priorities — and do so now. Until we stop chasing empty promises and start demanding real answers, we will remain captive to political manipulation.

Moreover, very few Zambians care whether an international plane lands in Mansa. But everyone in Mansa cares about roads, toilets and water. Those are the issues that should dominate our politics — not reserves, not airport vanity projects, and not slogans.

Besides, what good is an international airport in Mansa when the town does not even have a single local minibus? Motorbikes are still the common mode of transport. Politicians should promise what can be delivered–drinking water in every town, repaired sewer systems, housing for the poor, and roads that actually work.

I still remember UPND minister Felix Mutati promising that the New Dawn government would launch a satellite within two years. It made headlines. Five years later, nothing. The Lusaka-Ndola road is still incomplete after four years, yet somehow there is confidence to promise international airports in every province in the next five years. Do these politicians take us for fools?

Did Zambians ask for the 10 airports or the politicians chose to promise again? Who said we want airports? One only has to look at the Lusaka and CB traffic congestions to see that the transport and logistics network is so bad. Remember the L400 and C400 which added 800km road stretch but the country still yearns and yawns for lots more!
