title: A Multitude of Zambians Endorse HH for a Second Term
summary: There are political moments that redefine an election, and there are moments that settle every lingering doubt. The UPND’s campaign launch was one such moment. What the nation witnessed was not merely another political rally but a massive public demonstration of confidence in Hakainde Hichilema a...
content:
There are political moments that redefine an election, and there are moments that settle every lingering doubt. The UPND’s campaign launch was one such moment. What the nation witnessed was not merely another political rally but a massive public demonstration of confidence in Hakainde Hichilema and the direction in which he has taken Zambia over the past five years. As Zambians, we recognize the impact of this leadership. This event showcased the strength of grassroots support, as individuals from all walks of life came together, emphasizing unity and a shared vision for the future. It was a gathering where hopes and aspirations were echoed, illustrating the collective desire for progress.

The sea of people, the deafening chants, and the electric atmosphere sent one unmistakable message: a substantial number of Zambians are prepared to renew President Hichilema’s mandate. The turnout was not manufactured by speeches or slogans alone; it reflected the enthusiasm of supporters who believe the country is moving in the right direction and that the work of rebuilding Zambia is far from complete. This commitment to progress is essential for all Zambians, as it reveals the public’s deep-rooted belief in a brighter future. The palpable energy present at the event was a testament to the hope and determination shared by many citizens who envision a prosperous Zambia.

Unlike many campaigns that rely entirely on promises, President Hichilema enters this election with a record that supporters point to with confidence. Free education has opened classroom doors for countless children whose dreams were once limited by school fees. The Constituency Development Fund has empowered communities to undertake projects that directly improve people’s lives. Social Cash Transfer programmes have continued to cushion vulnerable families, while debt restructuring restored international confidence in Zambia and created room for economic recovery. These initiatives have transformed the lives of many, providing opportunities for growth and development, and demonstrating the government’s commitment to equitable progress.

These achievements are not isolated successes. Together, they form the foundation of a government that supporters believe has demonstrated both vision and discipline. A second term is necessary not to begin development but to accelerate it. The first five years laid the foundation. The next five years should deliver even greater economic expansion, job creation, industrial growth, and infrastructure development. The potential for Zambia to emerge as a regional leader is palpable, and the focus on long-term sustainability will ensure that progress is inclusive and far-reaching, benefiting all segments of society.

The campaign launch also highlighted something equally important: confidence. A government that enjoys public confidence campaigns differently. It does not appear defensive or uncertain. It stands before the people asking to be judged on its record. That confidence was evident throughout the launch as thousands gathered voluntarily to declare their support for President Hichilema. The atmosphere was charged with optimism, with citizens expressing their aspirations for a future defined by stability and growth. This sense of assurance is crucial in rallying continued support, as it embodies the trust Zambians have in their leadership.

Those expecting a closely contested political battle may need to reconsider the mood emerging from the ground. The opposition remains entitled to campaign vigorously and present alternative ideas, but it now faces the enormous task of convincing voters that it offers a better future than a government citizens believe has already delivered measurable progress. The contrast between the ruling party’s achievements and the opposition’s proposals will play a vital role in shaping voter perceptions as the election approaches. Many citizens feel a sense of pride in what has been accomplished and are wary of shifting gears mid-course.

For many citizens, this election is no longer simply about changing governments. It is about deciding whether to continue a programme of reforms that has already produced tangible results. They see continuity as the safest path toward sustained economic stability, expanded opportunities, and long-term national development. The narrative surrounding this election is not merely one of politics; it is deeply intertwined with the lives of ordinary Zambians who yearn for a better future. This focus on continuity versus change is essential for understanding the stakes involved.

If the energy, organisation, and public enthusiasm displayed at the launch are replicated across the country in the coming weeks, then the road to State House appears increasingly favourable for President Hakainde Hichilema. The signs emerging from the campaign trail suggest that many Zambians have already made up their minds. They are not merely attending rallies; they are signalling their intention to endorse the President with another mandate. This unwavering support reflects a collective understanding of the challenges ahead and the need for experienced leadership to navigate them effectively, reinforcing the belief that progress is achievable with the right guide.

Should that momentum carry through to election day, a decisive victory would not come as a surprise. For many observers, the campaign launch was more than a successful political event. It was an unmistakable declaration that a large section of the electorate believes the journey that began in 2021 deserves another five years to reach its full destination. Zambia must move forward and not in dununa reverse. The path forward, illuminated by the collective aspirations of the citizens, promises not only a continuation of progress but also an opportunity to address the deeper issues facing the nation. Zambians are ready for a future filled with hope and possibility.

In summary, this election represents a pivotal moment for Zambia. With the potential for transformative change and the opportunity to continue on a path of progress, the outcome will significantly influence the nation’s trajectory. Zambians are called to action to participate actively in the democratic process, ensuring that their voices are heard and their choices matter. The future of Zambia is in their hands, and it is a future that all can contribute to shaping together. Ultimately, the call to vote is not just a civic duty; it is a responsibility to oneself and to the generations that will follow.

Moreover, it is essential for the media to provide balanced coverage, highlighting both achievements and the critiques of the current administration. This transparency fosters a political environment where voters can weigh the pros and cons effectively. Engaging the youth in the electoral process is also vital, as they represent the future of the nation. Initiatives aimed at increasing youth voter turnout can significantly influence the outcome, shaping a Zambia that reflects the aspirations of all its citizens.

As the election date approaches, it is crucial for citizens to engage in informed discussions about the future of their nation. The stakes are high, and every vote counts. Zambians can be encouraged to educate themselves about the implications of their choices, ensuring that they are not just voting based on emotions but on a comprehensive understanding of the issues at hand. Grassroots movements and community forums can play a pivotal role in facilitating these conversations, empowering citizens to make decisions that resonate with their vision for Zambia.

title: Zambia pledges stronger economic ties with Burundi -Kawana
summary: Government has reaffirmed its commitment to deepen economic cooperation with Burundi, saying there is considerable potential to expand trade, investment, agriculture, mining, energy and tourism between the two countries.
content:
Government has reaffirmed its commitment to deepen economic cooperation with Burundi, saying there is considerable potential to expand trade, investment, agriculture, mining, energy and tourism between the two countries.

Speaking during a reception to mark the 64th Independence Anniversary of the Republic of Burundi, Ministry of Information and Media Permanent Secretary, Thabo Kawana, said Zambia values its longstanding ties with Bujumbura.

Mr Kawana noted that Zambia and Burundi’s relations are anchored on mutual respect, solidarity and a shared commitment to African unity.

He said both countries continue to work together within the African Union and other regional frameworks to promote peace, stability and sustainable development.

“Zambia remains committed to strengthening economic ties with Burundi through enhanced trade, investment, agriculture, tourism, mining, energy and infrastructure development,” he stated.

The Permanent Secretary welcomed the recent bilateral agreements signed between Lusaka and Bujumbura, describing them as a solid framework for deeper collaboration.

“Zambia therefore, looks forward to the full and timely implementation of these agreements, which will undoubtedly further strengthen economic cooperation, through increased trade and investment, as well as promote greater people-to-people exchanges,” Mr Kawana said.

He further commended Burundi’s efforts toward economic transformation and said stronger cooperation would help both countries meet national development goals and the broader aspirations of regional integration under the African Union’s Agenda 2063.

Mr Kawana proposed a toast to the good health of Burundi’s President Évariste Ndayishimiye, to continued Zambia Burundi cooperation and to the success of joint aspirations for enhanced regional integration.

title: Copper Queens clinches KCM sponsorship
summary: The government says Konkola Copper Mines’ new partnership with FAZ signals a “new dawn” for Zambian football, with women’s football set to benefit most.
content:
The government says Konkola Copper Mines’ new partnership with FAZ signals a “new dawn” for Zambian football, with women’s football set to benefit most.

Speaking at the signing ceremony in Lusaka on July 3, Permanent Secretary for Youth, Sport and Arts Kangwa Chileshe said the deal was a testament to private sector confidence under President Hakainde Hichilema’s administration.

“This event is not merely a celebration of partnership… it is a powerful testament to the shared vision of progress,” Chileshe said.

He noted KCM’s history of football support, from sponsoring the FAZ Super League from 2005 to 2011, to backing clubs like Nchanga Rangers, Konkola Blades and Aguila Stars.

Chileshe singled out KCM’s sponsorship of Nchanga Queens, Konkola Queens and Aguila Stars Women as “perfectly aligned with our policy focus on inclusivity and gender equity.”

He said the new sponsorship of the Copper Queens and a planned Women’s League Cup competition was “timely and transformative.”

“Our women’s national teams have consistently made us proud… yet, for too long, corporate engagement has not reflected this stellar success,” he said.

Chileshe urged other corporates to follow KCM’s lead, citing opportunities in Copper Queens travel kits and the Chipolopolo’s 2027 AFCON qualifiers.

He concluded: “This is the New Dawn in action; where vision, partnership, and sport converge to build a brighter future for all Zambians.”

Present at the event were Mines PS, National Sports Council GS, FAZ Vice President Mutale N’gandu and General Secretary Charles Chakatazya, and KCM Executive Director._

title: “Kabesha, Ubepelefye!” Is this the Beginning of HH’s Downfall?
summary: When Edgar Lungu’s sister told Zambia’s Attorney General in Pretoria, “Kabesha, ubepelefye” (you’re kidding!), UPND officials and President Hakainde Hichilema dismissed her threat—they believed they held all the cards regarding Lungu’s body. Didn’t the Vice President say, “don’t talk about the pe...
content:
When Edgar Lungu’s sister told Zambia’s Attorney General in Pretoria, “Kabesha, ubepelefye” (you’re kidding!), UPND officials and President Hakainde Hichilema dismissed her threat—they believed they held all the cards regarding Lungu’s body. Didn’t the Vice President say, “don’t talk about the person who was defeated, you want him to be defeated in death again?” Indeed, he who laughs last, laughs loudest. Esther Lungu, in her black dress, just humiliated the entire Zambian government machinery. Lungu just defeated President Hichilema in front of the entire world.

While President Hichilema has “defined” justice for countless individuals, he has realized for the first time that true justice cannot be bought. There still exist judges and lawyers willing to humiliate bullies in the public eye. Unlike the Zambian courts he controls, Pretoria told him that he does not define or control justice. Is this the beginning of his downfall?

I believe Mr. Kabesha lacks judgment of an Attorney General. The leaked audio exposing a plot by President Hichilema and Robert Chabinga to corrupt the South African justice system, and the scheme to have the South African government intervene in court proceedings should have immediately forced the AG to withdraw this case.

Alas, it was a script written for failure. Unlike in Zambia, in South Africa, some judges still have the decency to hold even their own presidents accountable. Here Kabesha was entering dangerous legal territory. In the end, the world watched as he was ordered not only to stop blocking the burial of Lungu, but also to pay all legal and related costs as the Lungus broke down in tears of joy. President Lungu can now rest in peace, despite his body being illegally opened by Hichilema’s legal team.

While the focus remains on Lungu’s victory, it is shameful for the AG to claim that the government won’t appeal—we know that the government lost. The Lungu legal team proved the unconstitutionality of the state’s claim on Lungu’s body under South African law. Mr. Kabesha has no room to appeal; it is a direct indictment of his legal acumen.

Honestly, Mr. Kabesha has disgraced Zambia to the world. By allowing a foreign court to rebuke him and order Zambia to foot the legal costs—alongside the massive bill for preserving Lungu’s body for over a year—he has exposed President Hichilema to public ridicule. The country will remember him as a man without a heart, fighting for a corpse that does not belong to him.

Furthermore, this debacle is an example of the waste of national resources. Why preserve the body for over a year? Why waste public funds on legal fees for a case the President could have resolved with the stroke of a pen? The people pleaded with him multiple times to discontinue this case, but pride comes before the fall.

Now that the Zambian government has exposed itself to a foreign jurisdiction, some financial consequences might follow in the same courts. Just as Kabesha was quick to sue, Zambia should now expect retaliatory lawsuits from the Lungu family for trauma, loss of income, and the illegal possession of the body, among other grievances. All of this could have been avoided had AG Kabesha realized that he is not the President’s personal lawyer, but the representative of the nation’s interests. Zambia had the chance to negotiate with the Lungus, yet it chose a fight that many knew it would lose.

Unfortunately, this case has political implications for Mr. Hichilema’s electoral messaging. While the UPND and Mr. Kabesha will try to reconstruct the narrative—claiming they went to court to secure a dignified burial for Lungu—there is only one real loser: President Hichilema.

Losing this battle weeks before the elections provides extra messaging momentum to Brian Mundubile and Makebi Zulu. They have beaten Hichilema once in Pretoria, and they will beat him again come August 13. Regardless of how the government attempts to reframe this, optics are everything in politics. Mr. Hichilema has been politically defeated by Makebi Zulu. Once again, the administration would have been wise to withdraw the case rather than suffer this public humiliation.

As a nation, we have much to learn from this saga. Aside from the unyielding strength of Mrs. Lungu, the case serves as an inspiration to countless widows across the nation. Fighting for your rights can be daunting, but you must never give up. Even the most powerful can be brought down. While President Hichilema may believe he is all-powerful and holds the state coffers, he is not invincible. He is beatable. Is this a testament of what is to come?

Persons like @ Spaka refuse to accept they holding onto a hot potatoe whether through ignorance or financial but certainly not in the interest of Zambia  and its people

When Zambians lined up the airport road to receive Lung’s remains, the powers chased them; when Zambians opened a book of condolences at Lungu’s residence, they opened theirs at Belvedere and blocked all other roads; when Lungu died, the powers doubted it, thinking he faked his own death to resurrect later for PLAN B, the powers forced their way to the South African morgue to mutilate his body to prove he was dead. The powers went to court NOT out of public interest, but for political self preservation.Besides, is it NOT “treason” to bury Lungu excluding Hichilema’s presence?

Dead or alive, Lungu remains a formidable political force in Zambia.

WELL STATEDThe grz in this instance does not represent the people but an individual and themselves

After that judgement in most civilised societies and the developed world ,Kabesha would have been a former attorney General by now. He knew very well that Zambia has no laws that can give the state any powers to usurp funeral and burial authority from a family of a former President but he went ahead to do wrong things.

There must be some consequences for such an incompetent President with his equally incompetent Attorney General after the Lungu burial case judgement. A very simple and straight forward case

Kabesha is a small time lawyer with a very small law firm in kabwe who has never dealt with high profile cases. The only reason he was appointed AG was because he used to visit HH when he was locked up in kabwe otherwise he knew there was no merit in the case against lungu family.

title: Campaign Messaging Fuels Debate Within Tonse Alliance
summary: Political campaigns are won not only on the strength of policies, but also on the discipline with which those policies are communicated. When campaign messaging begins to attract as much attention as the campaign itself, questions inevitably arise about strategy and direction.
content:
Political campaigns are won not only on the strength of policies, but also on the discipline with which those policies are communicated. When campaign messaging begins to attract as much attention as the campaign itself, questions inevitably arise about strategy and direction.

Reports attributed to individuals said to be aligned with Makebi Zulu’s camp within the Tonse Alliance suggest growing unease over the Alliance’s campaign messaging. According to these sources, some members are concerned that a series of controversial public statements on economic matters could affect the Alliance’s credibility and investor confidence.

If those reports are accurate, the concerns are understandable. Elections are fought as much on confidence as they are on promises. Markets, businesses and voters alike pay close attention to the signals presidential candidates send, particularly on issues touching the economy.

The same sources further claim that Mr Zulu favours a more measured, policy-driven approach and that some members are calling for greater message discipline. They also allege there is internal speculation among certain members that Mr Zulu would make a more credible presidential candidate than Mr Brian Mundubile. Whether those claims reflect a genuine mood within the Alliance or merely the views of a few individuals is something only the Alliance itself can clarify.

The reported unease comes against the backdrop of Mr Mundubile’s own public statements. He has said that, if elected, his administration would seek greater State control over privately owned mines and would utilise the country’s foreign exchange reserves for public benefit. Those remarks have generated considerable public discussion and differing interpretations of what such policies would mean in practice.

Political parties are free to advance whatever economic vision they believe best serves the country. Equally, they carry the responsibility of communicating that vision in a manner that reassures rather than unsettles. If the reported concerns within the Tonse Alliance are indeed genuine, they may simply reflect an appreciation that, in politics, how a message is delivered can sometimes prove just as important as the message itself.

There are no concerns within the Tonse Pamodzi alliance, and all is going extremely well and as per plan. The distracting message could be from the scared party

title: A Funeral, a Family, and a Nosy Government Mistaking Protocol for Law
summary: Picture this: a former President of Zambia dies while in South Africa. The nation mourns. Government officials dust off the state-funeral playbook, polish the ceremonial shoes, and begin preparing the speeches. Meanwhile, the family folds its arms and says: “Hold on. Before he was a former Presid...
content:
Picture this: a former President of Zambia dies while in South Africa. The nation mourns. Government officials dust off the state-funeral playbook, polish the ceremonial shoes, and begin preparing the speeches. Meanwhile, the family folds its arms and says: “Hold on. Before he was a former President, he was our husband, father, brother, and relative.” And just like that, a legal fight was born.

The matter landed before a South African court, which had to answer a surprisingly simple question: who gets the final say over President Lungu’s remains — the family or the State?

The Court’s answer was short, sharp, and powerful: the family.

Why? Because the dispute was being decided under South African law, and South African common law has long treated burial as a family matter. The judges essentially said that when a person dies, Government does not suddenly acquire ownership rights over the body like a neighbor claiming your bicycle simply because he admired it for years. Unless there is a law saying otherwise, or a valid agreement saying otherwise, burial decisions belong to the family.

The Zambian Government argued that President Lungu was no ordinary citizen. He was a former Head of State. National interests, protocol, and ubuntu, it said, justified a state-led burial.

The Court’s response was simple: “Fine sentiments. But where is the law?”

And that was precisely the problem. No convincing evidence was produced showing that Zambian law, custom, or protocol automatically gives Government the right to take control of a former President’s burial. The Court reminded everyone that foreign law must be proved by evidence, not by assumptions, emotions, or patriotic enthusiasm.

Government then tried another route. It argued that the family had already agreed to bring the body back to Zambia and allow a state funeral. But the Court found that the parties had merely been negotiating. And every lawyer knows the difference: talking about a deal is not the same thing as making one. Looking at the menu does not mean you have ordered the nshima.

The family’s position from the beginning was that President Hichilema should not participate in the funeral. The Court found no convincing evidence that they had ever changed their minds.

But hidden beneath the courtroom drama was a much bigger lesson. Governments are supposed to be creatures of law. Their powers should come from statutes and legal rules, not from wishful thinking dressed up in ceremonial attire.

Yet this case exposed a troubling gap. Government pressed forward without pointing to a clear law explaining what happens when a former President dies. Instead, reliance appeared to be placed on protocol, custom, and judicial remarks that were, at best, obiter dicta: “observations made in passing rather than the actual legal foundation of a previous decision.”

That is a bit like trying to build a house using the architect’s jokes instead of his blueprint. The jokes may be funny. They may even contain wisdom. But they will not stop the roof from collapsing.

And that is the real significance of this case. It reveals the urgent need for Parliament to enact a clear law governing the funerals, honours, and burial arrangements of former Presidents and other senior public officials. Questions of such importance should not be left to guesswork, negotiations, political pressure, or courtroom battles after emotions have already boiled over.

A country cannot comfortably navigate matters of national importance on the shaky scaffolding of implication and assumption. Sooner or later, somebody falls through the floor.

WHAT DOES THIS MEAN FOR ORDINARY CITIZENS?

Strictly speaking, this judgment does not determine the rights of Zambians under the Zambian Constitution. It is a South African judgment applying South African law. But it does illustrate a principle recognized in most constitutional democracies: if Government claims a special power, it must point to a law that gives it that power. “Because we have always done it this way” is a tradition. It is not a statute.

More importantly, the case serves as a warning for Zambia. It exposes the uncertainty that arises when a country lacks a clear legal framework governing the death, funeral, and burial arrangements of former Presidents. Without such legislation, future disputes risk becoming contests of politics, protocol, and public sentiment rather than questions answered by law.

And for the Lungu family, the judgment meant that the Court recognised their right, under the law it was applying, to honour President Lungu’s wishes and remain in control of his final journey.

The State may organise ceremonies, but it cannot rewrite rights simply because the deceased once occupied the highest office in the land. And where the law has left a vacuum, the answer is not improvisation by government officials but legislation by Parliament. Because in a nation governed by law, even the grandest funeral procession must march behind the law, not ahead of it.

Disclaimer: My commentary on this decision is no more a legal critique than a campfire tale is a treatise on thermodynamics. It is, rather, a dramatized retelling, a lively reenactment if you will, of the judicial clash, unburdened by the solemn drudgery of analysis and delivered with the unapologetic zest of a storyteller who knows a good duel when he sees one. The views expressed are solely my own and do not reflect the editorial position of Lusaka Times.

State funerals in the United StatesDownload PDIn the United States, state funerals are the official funeral rites conducted by the federal government in the nation’s capital, Washington, D.C., that are offered to a sitting or former president, a president-elect, high government officials and other civilians who have rendered distinguished service to the nation.[1][2] Administered by the Military District of Washington (MDW), a command unit of the Joint Force Headquarters National Capital Region, state funerals are greatly influenced by protocol, steeped in tradition, and rich in history. However, the overall planning as well as the decision to hold a state funeral, is largely determined by the family of the honoree, upon invitation by the government.[3]

State funerals in the United States

In the United States, state funerals are the official funeral rites conducted by the federal government in the nation’s capital, Washington, D.C., that are offered to a sitting or former president, a president-elect, high government officials and other civilians who have rendered distinguished service to the nation.[1][2] Administered by the Military District of Washington (MDW), a command unit of the Joint Force Headquarters National Capital Region, state funerals are greatly influenced by protocol, steeped in tradition, and rich in history. However, the overall planning as well as the decision to hold a state funeral, is largely determined by the family of the honoree, upon invitation by the government.

title: Magistrate sentences 2 for trafficking in cannabis, suspends sentence
summary: The Chisamba Magistrate court has sentenced two people to 12 months imprisonment with hard labour for trafficking in cannabis but suspended the sentence for two years.
content:
The Chisamba Magistrate court has sentenced two people to 12 months imprisonment with hard labour for trafficking in cannabis but suspended the sentence for two years.

Resident Magistrate, Susan Magalashi, convicted Gift Munkombwe and Ackson Zimba, both aged 37 and residents of Ten Miles area in Chisamba district in Central Province following their plea of guilty.

Magistrate Magalashi suspended the custodial sentences of the convicts under Section 39 of the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act of 2021.

Munkombwe was facing one count of trafficking in 2.1 kilograms (kg) of cannabis while Zimba was separately charged with trafficking in 1.2 kgs of marijuana, contrary to the laws of Zambia.

Public Prosecutor, Lydia Nyoka, earlier told the court that the seized substance tested positive for cannabis, containing a delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) with a concentration of 1 percent and 0.98 percent, which exceeds the legal potency limit of 0.3 percent.

Particulars of the offence are that on May 22ndand 23rd2026, Munkombwe and Zimba trafficked in 2.1 kgs and 1.2 kgs of cannabis respectively on separate occasions without lawful authority.

Following a search, Drug Enforcement Commission (DEC) officers from Kabwe arrested the duo after uncovering seven sachets of marijuana and four pre-packs of the drug in their possession.

The search also revealed an additional nine sachets of marijuana, a large pre-pack, and loose vegetable matter.

“On 22ndMay 2026 and 23rdMay, 2026, Drug Enforcement Commission (DEC) officers based in Kabwe conducted an operation at Ten Miles area after a tip off from members of the public,” facts read in part.

Magistrate Magalashi found each of the two accused guilty after they entered a guilty plea and admitted the facts.

After being found guilty of the offences, Munkombwe and Zimba pleaded for leniency and asked the court for mercy, saying that sending them to prison would unjustly punish their families.

“It is illegal in our country to possess marijuana and warrants a minimum mandatory sentence of up to a maximum sentence of 25years,” Magistrate Magalashi said.

Delivering her judgment, Magistrate Magalashi sentenced each of the accused to 12 months imprisonment with hard labour but suspended the sentence for 24 months.

Magistrate Magalashi said the sentences are suspended on condition that the convicts remain law-abiding citizens during the probation period and do not commit further offenses.

The court warned that if the convicts reoffend within their sentence period, they will serve their original terms plus an additional ten years.

Meanwhile, Magistrate Magalashi has ordered DEC officers to destroy the seized cannabis in the presence of the clerk of court.

title: Fuel Prices Drop as ERB Announces July Pump Price Cuts
summary: Zambian motorists will begin July with a welcome reduction in fuel prices after the Energy Regulation Board (ERB) announced lower pump prices for all major petroleum products, citing a decline in international oil prices and a marginal appreciation of the Kwacha against the United States Dollar.
content:
Zambian motorists will begin July with a welcome reduction in fuel prices after the Energy Regulation Board (ERB) announced lower pump prices for all major petroleum products, citing a decline in international oil prices and a marginal appreciation of the Kwacha against the United States Dollar.

The revised prices, which take effect from midnight on June 30, mean motorists filling up their vehicles and businesses dependent on fuel-powered transport will spend less at the pump over the coming month.

Under the new monthly review, petrol has been reduced by K1.00 per litre from K27.15 to K26.15, while diesel records the biggest monetary reduction among the widely used fuels, falling by K4.00 per litre from K32.11 to K28.11. Kerosene has been cut from K33.91 to K28.32 per litre, representing a reduction of K5.60, while Jet A-1 fuel has dropped from K36.68 to K30.27 per litre, down by K6.41.

The latest adjustment comes after international petroleum markets softened during the review period despite continued geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. According to the ERB, the average international price of petrol declined from US$124.24 per barrel to US$105.82 per barrel. Diesel also recorded a substantial reduction, falling from US$155.64 to US$125.65 per barrel, while Kerosene and Jet A-1 prices eased from US$196.56 to US$158.97 per barrel.

The regulator said movements on the foreign exchange market also worked in Zambia’s favour during the review period. The Kwacha strengthened slightly against the US Dollar, appreciating from K18.42 to K18.26 per US Dollar. Although modest, the currency gain complemented the decline in international oil prices, allowing the regulator to pass the benefit on to consumers through lower pump prices.

For motorists, the announcement offers some welcome relief after months of fluctuating fuel costs that have affected transport expenses for households, businesses and public service operators. Commercial transporters, farmers, manufacturers and logistics companies, all of whom rely heavily on diesel, stand to benefit from the largest reduction among the commonly consumed fuels.

Lower fuel prices also have the potential to ease operating costs across several sectors of the economy, particularly transport and distribution, although the extent to which those savings are passed on to consumers will depend on individual businesses and market conditions.

The July review demonstrates the ERB’s monthly pricing mechanism, which reflects movements in both international petroleum markets and the exchange rate rather than fixed domestic pricing. When global oil prices fall and the local currency strengthens, consumers generally benefit through lower pump prices. Conversely, increases in either factor can push domestic fuel prices higher.

While geopolitical tensions in the Middle East continue to create uncertainty in global energy markets, the regulator noted that international oil prices generally trended downward during the review period, providing the main basis for the latest reductions.

The new national uniform pump prices are:

The revised prices will remain in effect until the next monthly fuel price review by the Energy Regulation Board. The regulator has also made the detailed wholesale and pump price build-ups for July available on its website.

title: Thousands turn up to listen to President Hichilema in Western Province
summary: President Hakainde Hichilema has urged the people of Kaoma district in Western Province to vote for all the United Party for National Development (UPND) candidates in order for his administration to continue developing the area and country at large.
content:
President Hakainde Hichilema has urged the people of Kaoma district in Western Province to vote for all the United Party for National Development (UPND) candidates in order for his administration to continue developing the area and country at large.

President Hichilema noted that voting for the opposition is denying the much needed social and economic development which the country needs.

The Head of State was speaking today in Kaoma when he addressed a mammoth rally held at the Kaoma Youth Resource centre grounds.

President Hichilema outlined some of the developments achieved under his administration such as employment of over 45,000 teachers, recruitment of thousands of health workers and inclusion of the aged under the Social Cash Transfer.

Others are bursaries under the education sector and the signing into law of the free education policy and the 20 percent NAPSA partial withdrawal for workers in the public service.

President Hichilema has since reassured Zambia of his administration’s commitment to continue reducing the cost of living as evidenced by the reduction in fuel pump prices.

“I urge you all to vote for UPND again for continued development. As a government, we are making a lot of progress on further lowering the cost of living, just two days ago, we reduced the price of diesel from K32 to K28,” he said.

President Hichilema further appealed to the electorate not to vote for independent candidates but instead vote for UPND candidates because independents allegedly do not have his blessings.

Speaking at the same event, UPND Alliance Chairperson, Charles Milupi, highlighted the development happening in the road sector in the country, including in Kaoma district.

Mr Milupi said the expansion, rehabilitation and construction of roads across the country is evidence that President Hichilema’s administration has a vision to interlink Zambia with other countries.

He added that President Hichilema wants to open Zambia to other countries for investment, which will in turn improve livelihoods.

Meanwhile, UPND Deputy Secretary General, Gertrude Imenda, noted that all those alleging that it is a waste of time to expand Mongu airport are liars.

Ms Imenda noted that Western Province has a lot to offer and having a larger airport will attract investments in the tourism and agriculture sectors.

And UPND National Youth Chairperson, Gilbert Liswaniso, urged the people of Kaoma to vote for President Hichilema and all UPND candidates in the district so that they continue to enjoy development taking place.

The ruling UPND has fielded Morgan Sitwala as its candidate in Kaoma Central and Luhamba Mwene for Mangango constituency respectively.

And in Luampa in the same province, President Hichilema asked residents to give the UPND another mandate in the forthcoming general elections to sustain the country’s development trajectory.

Speaking to a huge crowd at Kasheke grounds, Mr Hichilema, said re-electing the UPND will safeguard the peace enjoyed in the past four years and protect the free education policy, which has now been enshrined into law.

The Head of State urged voters to support UPND candidates at all levels, warning against returning to past regimes that subjected citizens to harassment.

“You want freedom or go back to those who used to beat you? Those who used to beat you have just changed the name of the party but they are the same people,” he said.

President Hichilema also said creation of a new constituency in Luampa district will double Constituency Development Fund (CDF) allocation in the district.

“Within the (CDF) we have money meant for school bursaries, grants and loans,” Mr Hichilema said.

Meanwhile, UPND Alliance Partner, Charles Milupi, asked the people in the area to retain the UPND in power saying this will enable the upgrade of the Luampa-Kaoma road to bituminous standard.

Mr Milupi said the process of upgrading the road commenced during his tenure as Minister of Infrastructure, Housing and Urban Development.

Meanwhile, UPND National Youth Chairperson, Gilbert Liswaniso, appealed for married women to be included in the Social Cash Transfer programme, while Luampa East aspiring parliamentary candidate, Makozo Chikote, urged residents to vote for the UPND to accelerate development in the area.

Zambia goes to general elections on August 13, 2026 and several other presidential candidates are conducting campaigns around the country.

The incumbent President is in the race with Howard Kunda of Zambia We Want Party (ZAWAPA), Fred M’membe of Socialist Party (SP), Brian Mundubile of National Reconciliation Party for Unity and Prosperity (NRPUP), Brian Mushimba of Organised People’s Party (OPP), Ackim Njobvu of Democratic Union (DU) and Richard Silumbe of Leadership Movement (LM)

Other presidential candidates are Kelvin Fube of Zambia Must Prosper (ZMP), Given Katuta of Movement for Economic Emancipation (MEE), Xavier Chungu of Liberal Democrat Party (LDP), Harry Kalaba of Citizens First (CF), Daniel Pule of Christian Democratic Party (CDP) and Richwell Siamunene of New Focus Party (NFP)

Over 8.7 million Zambians have been registered as voters and are expected to cast their votes in the Presidential, National Assembly, Mayoral, Council Chairperson and Ward Councillor elections on August 13, 2026.

title: Hichilema Tells North-Western Voters Independent Candidates Will “Steal” His Votes
summary: PresidentHakainde Hichilemahas urged residents of Mwinilunga and Solwezi to reject independent parliamentary and council candidates, saying opposition parties are using them to weaken his parliamentary support ahead of the national vote.
content:
PresidentHakainde Hichilemahas urged residents of Mwinilunga and Solwezi to reject independent parliamentary and council candidates, saying opposition parties are using them to weaken his parliamentary support ahead of the national vote.

He said opposition parties were encouraging independent candidates to split the UPND vote and reduce the ruling party’s strength in Parliament. The President also appealed to former UPND supporters who had shifted their support to independent candidates to return to the party, saying national development required a united mandate.

President Hichilema linked continued UPND representation in Parliament and local authorities to programmes already introduced by his administration, including free education, increased Constituency Development Fund allocations and social cash transfer programmes. He also referred to the proposed constitutional reforms under Bill 7, saying they would create additional constituencies and improve representation.

Infrastructure development featured prominently in his address, with the President acknowledging the poor condition of several roads in North-Western Province. He identified the Mutanda-Mwinilunga Road, the Mutanda-Lumwana Road, the Kalumbila-Kisasa-Mwinilunga Road and the Mwinilunga-Ikeleng’i-Jimbe Road as priority projects under his administration.

According to the President, two contracts have already been signed for rehabilitation works on the Mutanda-Mwinilunga Road, while additional road projects would be undertaken as resources become available.

President Hichilema said his administration inherited a bankrupt Treasury and had therefore been unable to implement every development commitment within a single term. He said government had instead focused on rebuilding the economy while gradually rolling out development projects across the country.

He thanked the people of Mwinilunga for remaining loyal to the UPND throughout the party’s 23 years in opposition, saying their support had made it possible for the government to introduce policies such as free education and expand development funding to communities.

The President also pledged to complete hospitals in Mwinilunga and Ikeleng’i districts as part of efforts to improve access to healthcare in the province.

Several members of other political parties, including the Tonse Alliance, defected to the UPND during the rally.

New Nation Party presidentNevers Mumba, who also addressed the gathering, described the coalition of former ruling parties now supporting President Hichilema as unprecedented.

Later in Solwezi, President Hichilema addressed a second campaign rally attended by thousands of supporters, where he highlighted his government’s record on security, debt restructuring, education and agriculture.

He said Zambia had regained fiscal stability after restructuring its external debt, allowing government to redirect resources towards national development. He also pointed to increased support for farmers through the Farmer Input Support Programme and renewed investment in the mining sector, particularly in North-Western Province.

The President described the turnout in Solwezi as a reflection of the longstanding relationship between the UPND and the people of the province, whom he credited for standing with the party during its years in opposition.

While acknowledging that many challenges remained, he said his administration was committed to improving living standards, expanding economic opportunities and completing ongoing development projects across the country.

Patriotic Front faction presidentMiles Sampatold the gathering that the country had made notable progress in restructuring its debt over the past five years and urged voters to support President Hichilema.

Former freedom fighter and UNIP faction leaderHenry Miyobasaid the introduction of free education had enabled many children from low-income families to access schooling.

United Prosperous and Peaceful Zambia presidentCharles Chandaalso endorsed President Hichilema, saying he supported the government’s commitment to national development.

Closing his address, the President urged citizens to safeguard the gains achieved since 2021 and continue supporting the UPND.

President Hichilema is expected to begin a campaign tour of Western Province today, with stops scheduled in Nkeyema, Kaoma, Lukulu, Limulunga, Kalabo, Sikongo and Mongu before concluding the visit on Saturday.

title: The Hichilema Trap Mundubile Is Setting for Himself
summary: When I hear Brian Mundubile pledge to “respect” President Hakainde Hichilema (HH) should the opposition win the upcoming elections, it takes me back to the day HH was sworn in. He made a similar promise to Edgar Lungu. It was that very pledge that led me to conclude the new administration would b...
content:
When I hear Brian Mundubile pledge to “respect” President Hakainde Hichilema (HH) should the opposition win the upcoming elections, it takes me back to the day HH was sworn in. He made a similar promise to Edgar Lungu. It was that very pledge that led me to conclude the new administration would blur the separation of powers between the Judiciary and State House. It seems our presidents believe they hold the unchallengeable power to decide who gets a pass and who does not—and this is exactly what Mundubile’s rhetoric proposes.

Saying “I will respect you” implies that a president can personally control the fate of their predecessor. To me, this is what is wrong with our politics–a former head of state should not live at the mercy of the incumbent! It is time we say no to this mindset. Mundubile seems to be suggesting that while Hichilema used the presidency to punish Lungu, he will use it to reward Hichilema. Both approaches are planted in the abuse of power. President Hichilema is a Zambian citizen, and his rights are not determined by the whims of the one who occupies State House. If he is found to have committed a crime, Mundubile should not interfere in the legal process—unless he wants to assure the nation that he intends to grant a pre-emptive pardon regardless of the circumstances. While granting a pardon falls within constitutional powers, doing so would set a bad precedent. It would imply that presidents are above the law. Is this the Zambia we want?

Campaigning is one thing, but reality is another. Assume an incoming administration discovers a sitting president defrauded Zambians of billions of dollars. Would that pledge of “respect” still hold?

I am puzzled by Zambian politicians—they simply do not learn. We are living in the information age; what you say today may haunt you tomorrow.

President Hichilema’s primary issue with many voters is that he forgot this simple fact.  People can retrieve any of his past speeches on their phones. To deny what he said in 2021 is to lie, which is why the President has struggled to explain his way out of the grand promises he made before taking power. I am certain he knew he was just playing politics at the time—after all, that is what politicians do. What he failed to envision, however, was the weight of public expectation. People voted for him based on those exact promises. He also promised to respect Lungu, but did he?

I am not like the analysts who prefer to bury their heads in the sand. President Hichilema’s excitement during his rise in popularity in 2021 drove him to make declarations that I believe he now finds difficult to defend.

To be fair, he did not single-handedly cause our current economic hardships; we are a developing nation with many structural issues. But as I noted back then, it is one thing to promise the eradication of “cadrism” when you have just won an election, and quite another when you realize you need those same cadres to secure the next term.

Likewise, it is easy to pledge massive economic shifts until you face reality. The moment Hichilema entered Plot 1, he realized that the only jobs a president can single-handedly create are within the civil service—the police, the army, nursing, and teaching—because the government writes those checks. Yet our young people graduate in various specialized fields. Broad employment heavily depends on the private sector, which the president does not control.

This is the Hichilema trap Mundubile is setting for himself should he win on August 13. The very reasons Zambians may use to vote Hichilema out of Plot 1 will be turned against Mundubile unless he learns from his predecessor’s missteps. Any incoming leader will have to confront the national debt, unfinished and mismanaged Constituency Development Fund projects, a ballooning civil service, unemployment, and our culture of corruption. So, when I hear Mundubile make grand promises about raising civil servant salaries, expanding infrastructure, eradicating poverty, and fixing youth unemployment, my question is simple: where will the money come from?

It sounds straightforward on the campaign trail, but in reality, our national tax base is small. Furthermore, the ability to borrow is limited by the nation’s credit standing. We also have massive, ongoing statutory obligations just to keep the daily state functions. Mostly, the math simply does not add up. Frankly, making unrealistic promises is putting a political noose around one’s own neck.

Neither her nor there? Allow voters to elect president on their own choice. The promise to protect the future former president HH is serious and based on law. Forgive and forget. Prerogative of mercy is a presidential power under Zambian law

I am surprised that a learned man can say this. Some of us have no PhDs, but we had Civics in grade 9. We were taught that Prerogative of mercy comes AFTER conviction and not before, and while it is exercised by the President, it is also based on recommendation from a body. Maybe, doctorates teach something else.

title: Make home count, Kanyemba urges U-17
summary: Zambia’s Under-17 Women’s National Team hosts Ethiopia on Saturday in the first leg of the final round of 2026 FIFA U-17 Women’s World Cup qualifiers at Levy Mwanawasa Stadium. Kick-off is 15h00.
content:
Zambia’s Under-17 Women’s National Team hosts Ethiopia on Saturday in the first leg of the final round of 2026 FIFA U-17 Women’s World Cup qualifiers at Levy Mwanawasa Stadium. Kick-off is 15h00.

The Copper Princesses are targeting a third consecutive World Cup appearance. Ethiopia arrived in Ndola on Thursday afternoon.

Head coach Carol Kanyemba said preparations were complete.

“As coaches we have done everything needed. We are ready for the game; it’s now up to the girls to execute,” she said after Friday’s session at Levy.

Kanyemba said Ethiopia would be a new test.

“This is the first time we are meeting Ethiopia. Teams are different year in year out and for them to reach this far is testament to their quality,” she said.

Zambia will look to use home advantage before the return leg in Addis Ababa.

“We are going into this game with a new focus, which is to beat this team at home and get maximum points with enough goals to shield the away fixture,” Kanyemba said.

She urged fans to turn up in numbers.

title: Understanding Zambia’s reserves beyond the political noise
summary: Reserves debate: what Zambia’s foreign currency holdings actually do, and why growing them is not the whole story
content:
Reserves debate: what Zambia’s foreign currency holdings actually do, and why growing them is not the whole story

Zambia’s foreign exchange reserves have become one of the defining arguments of the election season, with two explanations now circulating that, read together, narrow the disagreement considerably rather than widen it.

Tonse Alliance spokesperson George Chisanga has clarified that Brian Mundubile has never called for Zambia to abandon its reserves. No country does away with reserves, Chisanga said, and growing them remains a major part of sound economic governance.

His argument, instead, is that growing reserves and growing the wellbeing of citizens carry equal weight. Neither should be sacrificed for the other, and reserves should not be presented as an achievement sufficient on its own to ask voters for their trust.

“Every government here has an obligation to build its reserves,” Chisanga said. “But you also have an obligation to ensure that the people who put you into power are well taken care of.”

He said the test is not whether reserves exist, but whether the people the reserves are meant to ultimately protect are seeing any benefit at all.

“It doesn’t make sense that somebody can be building reserves and bragging about them when people are living in squalor,” Chisanga said.

He pointed to suppliers unpaid for long periods and civil servants receiving salary increments of only 50 to 100 kwacha, calling the pattern a misplaced priority rather than evidence of fiscal discipline.

A separate explanation, from economic commentator Ntiumunya Matongo, approaches the same reserves from a more technical angle, focused on mechanics rather than politics.

Matongo described reserves as Zambia’s emergency savings account, built mainly in US dollars and other internationally accepted assets, used to pay for essential imports such as fuel, medicines and machinery while supporting confidence in the kwacha.

“There is a difference between protection and improvement,” Matongo said. “Reserves exist for protection.”

She compared reserves to a seatbelt, noting that they do not make a journey faster but protect against catastrophe if something goes wrong.

Matongo said the public often expects reserves to translate directly into personal financial improvement, but argued economies move through chain effects rather than direct ones. A stronger reserve position can support kwacha stability, which can ease import prices, which can lower borrowing costs, which can encourage investment, which eventually creates jobs and income.

She said this process, known as transmission, takes time, which is why government and citizens can describe the same economy in very different terms without either being wrong.

Matongo also pointed to a category of benefit that rarely gets noticed: the crises that never happen. Reserves that help Zambia avoid a currency collapse, or keep fuel supplies steady during a global price shock, rarely generate public recognition, because the public never experiences the disaster that was avoided.

Read together, the two explanations are closely aligned rather than opposed. Chisanga accepts that reserves are necessary and that no government should abandon them. Matongo accepts that the benefits of reserves take time to reach households and explains the mechanism behind that delay.

Where they differ is on what conclusion follows. Chisanga argues that government should not treat a rising reserve figure as a political accomplishment while citizens go without, calling for a social safety net to run alongside reserve growth. Matongo argues the lag in benefit is a structural feature of how economies function, regardless of which government is managing them.

Both perspectives converge on the same underlying point: reserves are a big chunk of economic governance, not the whole of it. The shared question they leave behind is what is being done to connect a strong national balance sheet to a household still waiting to feel any difference, a question likely to keep shaping how both government and opposition messages are received as campaigning continues toward the polls.

I am not going to comment on this because in the first place I always doubt what politicians say, meaning I doubt if this quantum of reserves really exist. I am not suggesting that the quantum is a lot but I just doubt it exists

content:
Ethiopia’s Under-17 Women’s National Team arrived in Ndola on Thursday ahead of Saturday’s 2026 FIFA U-17 Women’s World Cup qualifier against Zambia.‎‎The 36-member delegation touched down at Simon Mwansa Kapwepwe International Airport at 12h00 and was received by FAZ Competitions Manager Loreen Mulomba.‎‎Zambia’s Copper Princesses will host Ethiopia in the first leg of the third and final round of CAF qualifiers at Levy Mwanawasa Stadium, with a place at Morocco 2026 at stake.‎‎Coach Carol Kanyemba’s side is in its second week of camp in Ndola before the return leg in Addis Ababa.‎‎Meanwhile, the Football Association of Zambia has  been calling on fans to turn up in numbers for the match.‎‎Zambia is chasing a third appearance at the global showpiece, after eliminating Rwanda, Uganda and DR Congo.‎‎FAZ has already opened electronic ticket sales ahead of the match.

“From the comfort of your home you can buy tickets using your mobile phone,” Tembwe said.‎‎Zambia have been in camp in Ndola for over two weeks. Kick-off is 15h00.‎‎The return leg is in Ethiopia next week.

title: Mongu Deserves a World-Class Airport – Jito Kayumba
summary: Special Assistant to the President for Finance and InvestmentJito Kayumbasays Mongu deserves a world-class airport, arguing that the project would unlock Western Province’s economic potential while advancing Zambia’s broader development agenda. He said discussions on major infrastructure should n...
content:
Special Assistant to the President for Finance and InvestmentJito Kayumbasays Mongu deserves a world-class airport, arguing that the project would unlock Western Province’s economic potential while advancing Zambia’s broader development agenda. He said discussions on major infrastructure should not be limited to the country’s traditional economic centres.

The presidential aide said provinces such as Western Province equally deserve modern transport infrastructure capable of driving investment and growth. “Places like Mongu in the Western Province don’t just need a modern, world-class airport, they absolutely deserve one,” he said.

According to the presidential aide, an international-standard airport would strengthen tourism by improving access to some of Zambia’s leading attractions. These include the Kuomboka Traditional Ceremony, the Zambezi floodplains and Liuwa Plain National Park, which continue to draw visitors from within and outside Zambia.

Direct regional and international flights, he said, would transform Mongu from a difficult-to-reach destination into a thriving tourism and investment hub. Better air connectivity would also integrate Western Province more closely into the national and regional economy while reducing travel times for businesses.

Such improvements, he argued, would create fresh opportunities for entrepreneurs, agro-processors and other investors operating in the province. The agriculture and livestock sectors would equally benefit from faster cargo services linking producers to domestic and international markets.

“A fully equipped airport with dedicated cargo handling would mean faster, more efficient movement of high-value goods,” he said. Improved logistics, he added, would strengthen trade while increasing the competitiveness of locally produced goods.

Beyond trade, the project is expected to generate employment during both the construction and operational phases. Investment in hotels, roads, retail businesses and other supporting infrastructure would also be encouraged as economic activity expands around Mongu.

The Special Assistant also rejected suggestions that certain parts of the country do not deserve major infrastructure investment. Describing such views as unfair, he said denying strategic projects to some regions deprives local communities of opportunities capable of transforming their economies.

“It is deeply insulting for anyone to claim that any place in our country ‘doesn’t deserve’ an airport,” he said. “Infrastructure is not a luxury reserved for a privileged few provinces, it is the very foundation of equality and national progress.”

In his view, investment in Mongu would strengthen Zambia’s overall economic prospects rather than benefit Western Province alone. The proposed airport was presented as part of a broader effort to spread infrastructure development beyond the country’s established commercial centres.

Meanwhile,Mutotwe Kafwayahas apologised for his earlier remarks questioning the need for an international airport in Western Province. The Tonse Alliance finance chairperson said he regretted the language he used and had withdrawn the statements.

Speaking during a phone interview on Bulozi TV’sBusile Good Morning Breakfast Show, Kafwaya said the remarks did not reflect the respect he holds for the people of Western Province and the Lozi Kingdom. He described his choice of words as inappropriate and offered an unconditional apology.

The apology followed criticism from theBarotse Royal Establishment, which demanded that Kafwaya retract his remarks and issue a formal written apology. Addressing the media at Namoo Kuta in Limulunga District, BRE NgambelaMukela Manyandosaid the comments were inconsistent with the “One Zambia, One Nation” principle.

Manyando said the remarks undermined Western Province’s aspirations for development. He added that the people of the province have waited long enough for meaningful development and should no longer be treated as an afterthought.

Bold step for Western Province: It’s encouraging to see a vision for a world-class airport in Mongu. Investing in Western Province’s air connectivity is investments in Zambia’s broader growth story.

title: Inflation at 6.5%, But Does It Feel Like It? Analyst’s Remarks Draw Support, Pushback
summary: By The Economist Churchill Luhanga
content:
By The Economist Churchill Luhanga

The latest announcement that Zambia’s annual inflation rate has eased to 6.5 percent has opened fresh discussion over whether official economic indicators reflect the pressures being experienced by households and businesses, after financial analyst Yusuf Dodia questioned the latest figures and linked his concerns to commercial bank lending rates.

Speaking in an interview in Lusaka, Mr Dodia challenged the inflation rate published by the Zambia Statistics Agency (ZAMSTATS), arguing that prevailing lending rates in the banking sector point to a much higher level of inflation than the official data suggests.

ZAMSTATS announced on Thursday that annual inflation had eased from 6.6 percent in May to 6.5 percent in June 2026, extending the gradual downward movement recorded over recent months.

Mr Dodia, however, maintained that commercial banks appear to be pricing risk very differently.

He noted that many commercial banks are currently offering loans at interest rates ranging between 20 and 25 percent. Based on what he described as the traditional practice of banks lending at roughly five percentage points above inflation, he argued that the banking sector is effectively operating as though inflation is closer to 20 percent than the officially reported 6.5 percent.

“Normally banks give loans at about five percent above inflation, meaning the banking system in Zambia has determined that inflation is running at 20 percent for them to get an interest rate of 25 percent,” Mr Dodia said.

He added that the gap between official inflation and prevailing lending rates suggests that financial institutions have limited confidence in the published inflation figure and that many businesses and consumers continue to experience economic conditions that differ from the official statistics.

His remarks quickly attracted varied responses from economists, finance professionals and members of the public, with many agreeing that households continue to feel pressure despite the decline in headline inflation.

Some commentators said the reduction from 6.6 percent to 6.5 percent does not necessarily mean goods have become cheaper.

Instead, they explained that inflation measures the pace at which prices are increasing. A lower inflation rate simply means prices are still rising, but at a slower rate than before.

Using a simple illustration, they noted that an item costing K100 a year ago would now cost about K106.50 under an annual inflation rate of 6.5 percent.

Others argued that many consumers continue to experience pressure because prices remain well above previous levels, even though the rate of increase has moderated.

Among those supporting Mr Dodia’s position was economist Adon Kalapula Banda, who said the observations deserved serious consideration.

Mr Banda described Mr Dodia as an accomplished economist and said commercial banks are highly strategic when assessing lending conditions.

He argued that lending rates should not appear excessively disconnected from a country’s reported inflation rate.

“Banks are in business and are very strategic when lending money to their clients,” Mr Banda said, describing the analysis as impartial.

Not everyone agreed with the conclusions drawn from commercial lending rates.

Financial commentator Roystrings Mutombo said the comparison between bank interest rates and inflation oversimplified how lending rates are determined.

He argued that while inflation forms part of the broader economic environment, commercial banks consider several additional factors before deciding what interest to charge borrowers.

Mr Mutombo explained that central banks establish the macroeconomic foundation through monetary policy and benchmark rates, but commercial banks subsequently adjust lending rates based on their own cost structures and commercial assessments.

According to him, banks must recover the cost of funds, account for the interest paid to depositors, cover operational expenses, assess the creditworthiness of borrowers, consider the duration of loans and respond to competition within the financial sector.

He said lending rates therefore reflect much more than inflation alone.

“The central bank sets the macroeconomic baseline, while commercial banks adjust actual rates based on market conditions and individual borrower risk,” Mr Mutombo explained.

He added that higher lending rates may result from elevated credit risk, operating costs, funding expenses or long-term lending uncertainty rather than indicating that banks have privately rejected the official inflation figure.

His comments suggested that a direct comparison between inflation and commercial lending rates may not fully capture how banks price loans.

The differing perspectives have highlighted the distinction between headline inflation, which measures the average movement in consumer prices across the economy, and the wider financial conditions faced by businesses seeking credit or households attempting to access loans.

While official inflation has continued to decline gradually, borrowing costs remain considerably higher, raising questions for many consumers about why access to credit has not become substantially cheaper.

The discussion has also underscored the difference between statistical measurements produced by national agencies and the everyday experiences of households whose spending decisions are shaped by food prices, transport costs, utility bills and the cost of borrowing.

For some, the latest inflation figures point to continued progress in slowing price growth.

For others, including Mr Dodia, the persistence of relatively high lending rates raises questions about whether the broader economy is experiencing the same improvement reflected in the official statistics.

Although the competing explanations approach the issue from different angles, they converge on one point: inflation figures and commercial lending rates measure different aspects of the economy, leaving room for continued discussion over how best to interpret Zambia’s current economic conditions.

title: New Copper Queen targets WAFCON place
summary: Copper Queens midfielder Blessings Zulu says her first senior call-up has been exciting after captaining Zambia’s Under-20 side.
content:
Copper Queens midfielder Blessings Zulu says her first senior call-up has been exciting after captaining Zambia’s Under-20 side.

The Zanaco Ladies player is in the Copper Queens’ provisional squad preparing for the Women’s Africa Cup of Nations.

“Preparations are going well and everyone is working hard as a team,” Zulu told _FAZ Media_.

“For me, being here for the first time, I am excited. I have adapted well and the senior players have welcomed us.”

Zulu said the number of local players in camp showed the strength of the domestic league.

“Most of us are playing in the same league. It is important because we have to know where we are going.”

The Copper Queens are in camp with 29 players.

Zambia moves to Algeria on July 13 for an international camp that includes friendlies against the hosts on July 19 and 22.

title: Power releases Asia bound Prince Mumba
summary: Super League champions Power Dynamos have announced the departure of four players following the expiry of their contracts and a transfer abroad.‎‎Vice-captain Prince Mumba has left to join an Asian club.‎‎He signed for Power in 2024.‎‎Former captain Aaron Katebe leaves after his contract ended. T...
content:
Super League champions Power Dynamos have announced the departure of four players following the expiry of their contracts and a transfer abroad.‎‎Vice-captain Prince Mumba has left to join an Asian club.‎‎He signed for Power in 2024.‎‎Former captain Aaron Katebe leaves after his contract ended. The defender joined the club in 2022.‎‎Strikers Musonda Kapembwa and Austin Muwowo have also departed after their deals expired.‎Kapembwa arrived in 2025 while Muwowo joined in 2023.‎‎Club CEO Gibson Chaloba thanked the quartet for their service.‎‎“We sincerely thank Aaron, Prince, Musonda and Austin. Each played a critical role in the success of our club,” Chaloba said. “We wish them success in their future endeavours.”‎‎Power Dynamos said it remains focused on strengthening the squad ahead of the 2026 MTN Super League season and the CAF Champions League.‎‎Here’s your Shumah transfer story edited for newspaper — tightened, fixed grammar, cut repetition:‎‎Last week, Kenyan striker Moses Shumah has left Power Dynamos to join South African club AmaZulu FC.‎‎Shumah left alongside midfielder Brian Masanyinga, who is joining the Zambia Police Service for training.‎‎Power assured supporters the club remains committed to building a competitive squad ahead of the new season and continental assignments.

title: Power Queens seal rapid rise
summary: LFive teams have secured promotion to the National League ahead of the 2026 transitional season after winning playoffs at Lilayi Grounds in Lusaka.
content:
LFive teams have secured promotion to the National League ahead of the 2026 transitional season after winning playoffs at Lilayi Grounds in Lusaka.

Forest Myooye, Mathkat United, Nampundwe, Play It Forward and Mikalile emerged from 10 provincial Division One champions.

Nampundwe are back in the National League after a long spell in lower divisions.

Coach Clive Mwansa said the club would strengthen ahead of the new campaign.

“The playoffs were tough. We will beef up the team to compete favourably in the National League,” Mwansa said.

The Lusaka-based club was formed in 1972.

Forest Myooye coach Evaristo Mkandawire said the side was ready for the step up.

“This is a dream come true and we dedicate this qualification to our fans and sponsors,” Mkandawire said.

“We know the National League is demanding, but we shall be ready to compete.”

In the women’s category, Young Green Buffaloes Girls, Kumm’awa Stars Queens, Power Dynamos Queens, Lunchu Queens, Barts Queens and Mwenzo Queens have also earned promotion.

Debutants Power Dynamos Queens were only formed in 2025. Coached by White Mwanambaba, they won the FAZ Provincial League to qualify for the playoffs.

title: Hakainde Hichilema, an achiever by any honest measure
summary: Zambia’s headline inflation rate fell to6.5 percent in June 2026, its lowest level since February 2018. Four and a half years ago it stood at22.8 percent. That single number tells a story that critics of President Hakainde Hichilema rarely want to sit with. President Hakainde Hichilema’s leadersh...
content:
Zambia’s headline inflation rate fell to6.5 percent in June 2026, its lowest level since February 2018. Four and a half years ago it stood at22.8 percent. That single number tells a story that critics of President Hakainde Hichilema rarely want to sit with. President Hakainde Hichilema’s leadership has been instrumental in this economic transformation.

The Constituency Development Fund now stands atK40 million per constituency. In 2021 it was K1.6 million. That is not a modest adjustment. It is a twenty-five-fold increase, delivered in stages, and it has paid for more than2,800 classroom blocks, 670,000 desks, 156ambulances and nearly1,800 boreholesacross the country.

Free education has returned more than2.3 million childrento classrooms who had previously dropped out. Examination fees, once a barrier for poor families, have been scrapped. Nearly42,000 teachershave been recruited since 2021, alongside thousands of health workers, the highest recruitment numbers in the country’s history.

Farmers have felt the shift too. The Food Reserve Agency now buys maize atK340 per 50-kilogramme bag, up from levels as low as K150 a few years ago. The 2025/2026 season saw production climb sharply, with government crediting the improved price for pulling more farmers, including in the north, into large-scale maize growing.

Social cash transfers, the safety net for the country’s most vulnerable citizens, have doubled and now reach1.5 million households. Zambia has also pushed through one of the most complex sovereign debt restructurings on the continent, with more than 90 percent of external debt now resolved and annual debt-servicing costs cut from roughly $2.3 billion to about$900 million,freeing money for exactly the programmes listed above.

None of this happened by accident. Zambia inherited a defaulted economy in 2021, the first African country to fail on its debt repayments during the pandemic. The kwacha has since become one of thebest-performing currencies in the worldfor extended stretches. Business Day ranked Hichilema fifth among Africa’s best-performing leaders. The Telegraph named him among its World Leaders of 2025, praising what it called a turnaround from economic basket case to a model of fiscal discipline.

Under the guidance of Hakainde Hichilema, Zambia has seen significant improvements in various sectors, showcasing a commitment to transformation and growth.

So the question stands. How do you call someone who dragged the country back from default a failure? How do you call someone who tripled teacher recruitment, doubled the safety net for the poorest households, and put nearly 2,800 classroom blocks on the ground a failure?

To be fair to the President himself, he has not claimed the job is finished. Addressing Parliament in February, Hichilema pointed to the inflation numbers and added a caution alongside the credit. Lower inflation benefits the nation, he said, but much work still lies ahead. He has gone further still, acknowledging plainly that many citizens have not yet felt the benefits in their own pockets, and that the cost of living needs to come down further before ordinary households notice the difference the statistics describe.That admission matters. A leader who claims to have solved every problem invites suspicion.

A leader who says inflation is falling, debt is being tamed, and classrooms are being built, while also admitting that the average Zambian has not yet felt the full weight of that progress, is describing the country as it actually is. Progress and hardship are not mutually exclusive. Zambia can be a country where inflation has fallenfrom 22.8 percent to 6.5 percentand where a mother in a market still finds mealie meal expensive. Both things are true at once, and pretending otherwise, in either direction, does the public no favours.

What the record does not support is the claim of total failure. A government that has rebuilt a defaulted economy, restored allowances that were previously withdrawn, expanded the safety net, doubled down on rural infrastructure and kept a currency stable enough to draw international attention is not a government that has done nothing. It is a government midway through a difficult repair job, by its own admission, with real results already visible and real work still ahead.

The opposition is entitled to demand faster relief on the cost of living. That is legitimate politics. What is not legitimate is pretending the last four and a half years produced nothing. The numbers, independently reported by the Zambia Statistics Agency, the IMF, the World Bank and international outlets from Bloomberg to The Telegraph, do not support that argument.

So once more, to those still insisting this presidency has achieved nothing: if debt restructuring, single-digit inflation, doubled social protection, a tripled teacher corps and thousands of new classrooms represent failure, what, precisely, would success have looked like?

title: Kalasa backs U-17 girls to book World Cup spot
summary: Veteran Ndola administrator Richard Kalasa says Zambia’s Under-17 Women’s Team can secure a third FIFA World Cup appearance when they face Ethiopia in Saturday’s qualifier at Levy Mwanawasa Stadium.
content:
Veteran Ndola administrator Richard Kalasa says Zambia’s Under-17 Women’s Team can secure a third FIFA World Cup appearance when they face Ethiopia in Saturday’s qualifier at Levy Mwanawasa Stadium.

Zambia and Ethiopia meet in the first leg of the final qualifying round for Morocco 2026. Carol Kanyemba’s side has already eliminated DR Congo, Uganda and Rwanda.

Kalasa, former Forest Rangers chairperson, said the team had shown quality in Ndola.

“I want to see the girls qualify for the World Cup. The last time I watched them at Levy against DR Congo, they showed massive talent,” Kalasa said.

“We have a future in these girls. They are young, talented players who will take over from Barbra Banda and Rachael Kundananji.”

The former administrator urged fans to turn out in numbers.

“I am encouraging all supporters to come to Levy Mwanawasa Stadium and back the girls,” he said.

Zambia have been in camp in Ndola for over two weeks. Kick-off is 15h00. The return leg is in Ethiopia next week.
