title: Andyford Banda Resigns as OPP Running Mate, Party Notifies ECZ
summary: According to a statement issued by the Organized People’s Party and signed by its Presidential Candidate Dr. Brian Mushimba, Andyford Banda has stepped down as the party’s vice-presidential running mate.
content:
According to a statement issued by the Organized People’s Party and signed by its Presidential Candidate Dr. Brian Mushimba, Andyford Banda has stepped down as the party’s vice-presidential running mate.

The OPP said it respects Banda’s decision and thanked him for his dedication to the party’s work so far.

“We fully respect his decision, thank him for his dedication to our shared journey, and wish him the very best moving forward,” the statement read.

The development comes at a sensitive time in Zambia’s electoral calendar, with the country preparing for the August 13, 2026 general election.

Changes to presidential tickets trigger legal and administrative processes, which is why the party says it has already moved to inform the relevant authorities.

As reported in the OPP statement, the party has officially notified the Electoral Commission of Zambia and asked for guidance on the next legal steps.

The party further stated that it will comply fully with the law and with any directives issued by the Commission.

“We will provide a formal update to the nation as soon as we receive further guidance from the Electoral Commission,” Dr. Mushimba said in the statement.

Who is Andyford Banda and what was his role in OPP

According to public party records and the OPP’s earlier announcements, Andyford Banda was unveiled as Dr. Mushimba’s running mate as the party sought to position itself as an alternative ahead of 2026.

The OPP has presented itself as a new political formation focused on what it calls credible, practical and principled leadership. Banda’s profile was intended to add balance to the ticket and broaden the party’s appeal across different voter groups.

While the statement did not give reasons for Banda’s resignation, such changes are not uncommon in election build-ups.

Parties often reshuffle tickets due to internal consultations, personal reasons, health, business commitments, or strategic repositioning as campaigns take shape.

What the law says about replacing a running mate

The Electoral Commission of Zambia oversees candidate nominations and substitutions under the Electoral Process Act and related regulations.

According to ECZ procedures used in past elections, once a presidential candidate and running mate are nominated, any change must be communicated to the Commission, which then advises on timelines, forms and compliance requirements.

Political analysts note that early notification gives parties room to manage the process without risking disqualification or administrative challenges.

By stating that it has already written to ECZ and will follow its guidance, the OPP is signaling intent to remain within the legal framework.

In the statement attributed to Dr. Brian Mushimba, the OPP said the party remains united and focused on its 2026 message to voters.

“Our party remains united, focused, and deeply committed to providing Zambians with the credible, practical, and principled leadership our country deserves. Our vision and our promises to the people of Zambia stand firm,” the statement said.

The party did not name a replacement for Banda. The next step will depend on ECZ’s guidance on nomination amendments, deadlines, and documentation. Once the Commission responds, the OPP is expected to announce a new running mate and proceed with campaign preparations.

The resignation comes as Zambia’s political landscape becomes more active ahead of 2026.

The ruling United Party for National Development is defending its record on the economy, debt restructuring, service delivery and cost of living.

The main opposition and smaller parties, including the OPP, are seeking to present alternatives on jobs, governance and constitutional issues.

For a newer party like the OPP, stability on the presidential ticket matters for credibility with voters and with stakeholders such as donors, civil society and the media.

A smooth transition, managed in line with ECZ rules, can help the party maintain momentum.

Communications contact provided by the party

For media and public inquiries, the OPP listed Francis Chipalo as its Media Consultant for Politics. According to the statement, Chipalo is the contact person for further queries on this matter.

Three issues will now be in focus. First, ECZ’s response and the legal timeline for replacing a running mate. Second, the profile of the person the OPP selects to fill the position, and how that choice affects the party’s regional and demographic balance. Third, how quickly the OPP can return to campaigning with a settled ticket.

As stated by the party, a formal update to the nation will follow once the Electoral Commission provides direction. Until then, the OPP says its policy positions and campaign commitments remain unchanged.

title: We have done alot, Hichilema seeks fresh mandate, makes more promises to North Western
summary: Hichilema seeking re-election in Mwinilunga
content:
Hichilema seeking re-election in Mwinilunga

MWINILUNGA, NORTH-WESTERN PROVINCE, July 1, 2026– President Hakainde Hichilema has appealed to Zambians to grant him another five-year mandate in next month’s general election, saying his administration has delivered significant development over the past five years but still has more work to accomplish.

Speaking during a campaign rally at Kaunda Square in North-Western Province on Wednesday, the United Party for National Development (UPND) presidential candidate said his government remains committed to improving infrastructure and strengthening the country’s economic foundations.

Among the key commitments made during the rally was the continued rehabilitation and construction of roads in the province, which he said remain a priority for his administration.

“Road infrastructure remains dear to us and we are determined to work on these and many other economic fundamentals,” President Hichilema told supporters.

The Head of State said while his government had made considerable progress since assuming office in 2021, additional time was needed to complete its development agenda.

“So much has been done but there is more to be done and this is why I ask you all to vote for us in next month’s election,” he said.

President Hichilema said the large crowd that attended the rally reflected the confidence that people in North-Western Province have in his leadership and their desire for continuity.

According to the President, his administration has focused on protecting democratic freedoms and creating a peaceful political environment in which citizens can freely participate regardless of political affiliation.

“Together, we shall continue making Zambia better for all as opposed to the past times when anyone who wore a red T-shirt was living under fear of attacks from political groupings,” President Hichilema said.

The President also urged supporters to turn out in large numbers on polling day and vote for the UPND, saying continuity would allow his government to consolidate the gains already achieved and accelerate national development.

Following the Kaunda Square rally, President Hichilema travelled to Solwezi, where he was scheduled to address another campaign rally as part of his two-day tour of North-Western Province ahead of the August general election.

title: Mundubile will fix it, he tells crowd in Mpulungu
summary: Tonse PF-Pamodzi Alliance presidential candidate Brian Mundubile holds Mpulungu rally on 3 July 2026, tells Northern Province voters he will fix transport, fishing and cost of living
content:
Tonse PF-Pamodzi Alliance presidential candidate Brian Mundubile holds Mpulungu rally on 3 July 2026, tells Northern Province voters he will fix transport, fishing and cost of living

Addressing a packed crowd at Mpulungu’s Independence Grounds on Thursday 3 July 2026, Mundubile said his government will address the pertinent issues facing citizens today.

The rally drew thousands of residents, fishermen, traders and boda boda riders from across Mpulungu, Mbala, Kasama, Senga Hill and Nsama districts.

Party officials described it as one of the largest political gatherings seen in the lake district this year.

Mpulungu, Zambia’s only port town on Lake Tanganyika, has long felt distant from Lusaka decision making. The turnout reflected both PF-Pamodzi mobilisation and local frustrations over high transport costs, erratic fishing incomes, poor roads, and rising food prices.

By midday, the grounds were full, with supporters standing on trucks, bicycles and perimeter walls to hear the candidate.

*The crowd and local significance*Mundubile arrived by road from Mbala and was met by drumming groups, fishermen in canoes along the shoreline, and market women in PF-Pamodzi chitenge. Traders closed Mpulungu Market early. Boat operators docked to attend. Youth groups from the border posts at Tunduma and Nsumbu were visible in the crowd.

The size of the gathering mattered politically. Northern Province has been a swing region in recent elections, and Mpulungu is a gateway for trade with Tanzania, Burundi and the DRC. A strong showing there signals capacity to mobilise along the corridor from Kasama to the lake and to the border.

*Mundubile’s main message*Speaking for just under 45 minutes, Mundubile tailored his address to Mpulungu’s economy. His speech centred on four themes: transport and port revival, fisheries and food security, cost of living, and youth jobs.

First, the port and transport corridor. He said Mpulungu must be treated as a national trade asset, not a forgotten town. He promised to rehabilitate the Mpulungu-Mbala-Kasama road, fix the ferry landing and cold chain facilities, and reduce clearance delays at the border. He argued that when the port works, fish, fuel, cement and groceries become cheaper because transport time and losses fall. He also pledged to push for dredging and safer jetty access so larger boats can dock even in low water.

Second, fisheries and food security. Lake Tanganyika supports thousands of households. Mundubile said PF-Pamodzi will work with fishing cooperatives to provide affordable gear, ice, storage, and market linkages to Kasama, Lusaka and export routes.

He committed to ending random fishing bans without consultation and to replacing them with science-based closed seasons agreed with communities. He also promised timely aquaculture support, fingerlings and feed subsidies, so young people can farm fish rather than only rely on wild catch.

Third, cost of living. He told the crowd that macro numbers do not help if a 25kg bag of mealie meal, cooking oil and fuel are still expensive. He linked high prices to bad roads, fuel levies and middlemen.

His plan, he said, is to cut transport costs through road repairs, reduce fertiliser and input delays for farmers in Nsama and Senga Hill, and expand local milling so Mpulungu does not depend entirely on grain trucked from far away.

Fourth, youth jobs and skills. He promised skills centres in Mpulungu and Mbala focused on boat building, engine repair, refrigeration, logistics, and border trade documentation. He said local content rules will require mining and infrastructure contractors in the province to hire and train local youth first.

*Accountability and delivery*Mundubile repeated his national message that government must be judged on delivery, not new promises. He argued that the UPND’s 2026-2031 manifesto repeats ideas from 2021 that, in his view, remain incomplete. He cited delayed FISP distribution, incomplete road works, and power outages that affect fish cold storage.

He pledged quarterly community scorecards in Mpulungu, Mbala and Kasama, with district officials required to report publicly on roads, health centres, and fisheries support. He said ministers who fail to deliver will be held to account.

*Response on the ground*The loudest applause came when he spoke about the port, fishing bans, and road repairs. Fishermen said erratic bans and lack of cold storage waste catch and reduce incomes. Market women said poor roads make tomatoes and vegetables rot before they reach Kasama. Boda boda riders said fuel prices eat their earnings. Mundubile’s promise to fix the corridor and consult on fishing rules landed directly on those concerns.

Several residents interviewed after the rally said they came because Mpulungu feels bypassed in national budgets. A fish trader said ice and storage would allow her to keep fish longer and negotiate better prices. A boat mechanic said a skills centre would help him service outboard engines instead of sending them to Dar es Salaam.

*Political context*The rally came days after President Hakainde Hichilema launched the UPND’s new manifesto with targets on power, copper, maize and tourism. Mundubile’s approach was to localise the debate. Instead of national production numbers, he spoke about the Mpulungu-Mbala road, jetty access, ice, and border clearance.

Policy overlap with UPND remains visible. Both sides speak about mining value addition, tourism, agro-processing and trade. Mundubile’s differentiation is tone and sequencing: consult first, fix transport and inputs first, publish quarterly reports, and prioritise household prices over abstract growth figures.

The Tonse PF-Pamodzi Alliance is still building out detailed, costed plans in public. For now, the alliance is relying on a message of delivery, accountability and corridor development to win Northern Province.

*What happens next*PF-Pamodzi officials said the Mpulungu event will be followed by meetings in Mbala, Kasama and Luwingu later in July. They expect to release provincial policy briefs on transport, fisheries and agriculture before August.

For Mpulungu voters, the immediate test will be whether promises on the road, port and fisheries turn into funded projects and visible work before the 13 August election.

For Mundubile, the Mpulungu crowd showed that lake communities will turn out when a candidate speaks directly to transport costs, fishing livelihoods, and food prices.

title: 2026 election presents Zambians with easiest choice- Hichilema
summary: President Hakainde Hichilema who is facing a strong challenge from Tonse PF-Pamodzi Alliance presidential candidate Brian Mundubile says the forthcoming general election set for August 13 presents Zambians with the easiest choice to make.
content:
President Hakainde Hichilema who is facing a strong challenge from Tonse PF-Pamodzi Alliance presidential candidate Brian Mundubile says the forthcoming general election set for August 13 presents Zambians with the easiest choice to make.

Addressing residents during a campaign meeting in Luampa, President Hichilema said Zambians would be faced with a clear choice at the polls, adding that his administration had made significant progress since assuming office.

“On 13 August, Zambians face a clear choice: the Zambia we are building together or the Zambia we left behind in 2021,” President Hichilema said.

Apparently, Mundubile and his running mate Makebi Zulu are pulling huge crowds at their rallies.

And critics say as the polls draw closer with each passing day, it is becoming clear that the forthcoming general election will be a two horse race.

Mundubile and Zulu’s campaign message is centered on reviving the late former president Edgar Lungu’s legacy.

Meanwhile, President Hichilema urged voters in Luampa to support all UPND candidates in the August 13 General Election, saying the country is at a crossroads between sustaining recent development gains and returning to the challenges that existed before 2021.

He said his government inherited a country burdened by debt, economic decline and insecurity but had since restored stability through policies aimed at improving livelihoods.

“In 2021, we inherited a country buried in debt, ruled by fear and cadreism, with an economy in decline.

“We thank our hardworking farmers in Luampa for responding positively to our policies and contributing to this year’s bumper harvest. Their dedication is helping to strengthen our nation’s food security and feed our nation,” he said.

The President acknowledged that despite the progress made, more work remained to be done.

“To protect the gains we have made and enable us to continue delivering development and expanding opportunities for every Zambian, we ask the people of Luampa to vote for all UPND candidates on the ballot,” President Hichilema said.

Hichilema said he expects the reduction in fuel prices to result in lower transport costs and, ultimately, reduced prices of goods and services across the country.

He also disclosed that the price of cement has fallen from K176 to K158 per bag and urged retailers to pass on the price reduction to consumers.

title: Mundubile Distances NRP-UP, Tonse Alliance from Kafwaya’s Airport Remarks, Pledges Development for Barotseland
summary: Brian Mundubile visiting Barotseland
content:
Brian Mundubile visiting Barotseland

ISOKA, July 1, 2026– National Reconciliation Party for Unity and Prosperity (NRP-UP) presidential candidate Brian Mundubile has distanced his party and the Tonse Alliance from remarks made by Tonse Alliance Chairperson for Finance, Mutotwe Kafwaya, regarding the need for an international airport in Western Province.

In a press statement issued from Isoka on Tuesday, Mundubile said he had taken time away from his campaign programme to clarify the alliance’s position following concerns raised over Kafwaya’s comments.

He thanked the Barotse Royal Establishment (BRE) for giving the NRP-UP an opportunity to explain its position, describing the gesture as a demonstration of the royal institution’s commitment to fairness and dialogue.

Mundubile said Kafwaya’s remarks reflected his personal opinion and not the official position of either the NRP-UP or the Tonse Alliance.

“I wish to make it absolutely clear that Hon. Kafwaya’s comments reflected his personal views and not the position of the NRP-UP and Tonse Alliance,” he said.

According to Mundubile, Kafwaya has since apologised for the remarks and has prepared an unconditional written apology withdrawing the statement.

The opposition leader further assured the BRE that no member of the alliance would campaign on a platform that seeks to deny development to any part of Zambia, including Barotseland.

Mundubile pledged that, if elected, his administration would work closely with the BRE to promote inclusive and consultative development in Barotseland and across the country.

He said his government would engage the BRE on policy proposals for the region during the alliance’s forthcoming campaign visit to Western Province.

The presidential candidate also criticised successive governments for what he described as unfulfilled promises made to the people of Barotseland, citing projects such as King Lewanika University and a stadium, which he said have not materialised despite previous commitments.

Mundubile further referred to remarks allegedly made by President Hakainde Hichilema in January 2024 regarding Barotseland, saying they had caused hurt among many people in the region.

Outlining some of his party’s proposals for Western Province, Mundubile said an NRP-UP government would prioritise road infrastructure, including the Mongu-Lusaka and Sesheke-Livingstone roads, invest in cattle production and the fight against foot-and-mouth disease, improve veterinary and agricultural extension services, raise education standards, and upgrade Kalabo and Mongu airports.

He said the party would work with the Barotse Royal Establishment to identify development priorities and ensure that policies reflect local needs.

“We promise to transform Barotseland. We promise to transform Zambia,” Mundubile said.

title: THE EYE VIEW: Are the Political Winds Shifting? Why UPND Cannot Afford to Underestimate the Mundubile–Makebi Challenge
summary: Mundubile and Makebi after filing nominations. Picture credit, Grindstone TV
content:
Mundubile and Makebi after filing nominations. Picture credit, Grindstone TV

By Mbanga Irvine IlukuiFayetteville, Arkansas, USA

As Zambia edges closer to the 13 August 2026 General Election, one lesson from the country’s political history continues to stand out: elections are often lost long before polling day by those who convince themselves that victory is already guaranteed.

Political overconfidence has undone governments before.

In 2021, many senior Patriotic Front (PF) officials dismissed warning signs that public sentiment had shifted. Even as economic hardships mounted and opposition rallies attracted growing crowds, confidence within the ruling party remained high until voters delivered a different verdict at the ballot box.

Whether history repeats itself remains to be seen, but similar questions are beginning to emerge in the current political landscape.

President Hakainde Hichilema and the UPND remain favourites in many political assessments. They continue to enjoy the advantages of incumbency, a nationwide party structure and control of government programmes.

However, recent developments suggest the opposition may no longer be as politically weak as many had predicted only a few months ago.

One of the dominant narratives following the death of former President Edgar Lungu was that Zambia lacked a serious opposition capable of mounting a meaningful challenge to President Hichilema.

That perception is increasingly being tested.

The emergence of the Tonse Pamodzi Alliance under Brian Mundubile, with constitutional lawyer Makebi Zulu as running mate, has injected new energy into opposition politics.

Large campaign rallies in Kitwe, Kabwe and parts of Eastern Province have attracted sizeable crowds despite comparatively limited financial resources and campaign infrastructure.

Crowd size alone does not determine electoral success. Political rallies are not votes.

However, they often provide an indication of enthusiasm, mobilisation capacity and public interest—three ingredients that matter in any election.

Another issue that may influence the presidential race is the growing number of independent parliamentary candidates who were previously members of the UPND but failed to secure adoption.

President Hichilema has repeatedly urged party members to support only officially adopted UPND candidates and has discouraged independents from campaigning in his name.

From a party discipline perspective, that position is understandable.

However, politically, some analysts argue it carries risks.

Independent candidates often retain strong grassroots networks. In constituencies where they command personal loyalty, their supporters may decide to vote for the independent parliamentary candidate while withholding support from the UPND presidential candidate.

Whether this translates into significant votes nationally remains uncertain, but it is a factor worth watching.

The opposition landscape entering the 2026 election differs significantly from that of 2021.

Before the last election, the PF entered the campaign deeply divided, with several influential figures operating outside the party.

This year, many of those previously competing political camps have found common ground within the Tonse Pamodzi Alliance or are publicly supporting Brian Mundubile’s presidential bid.

Although individual parties retain their identities, the broader objective of challenging the UPND has created a level of cooperation that did not exist five years ago.

Whether that unity survives the pressures of a national campaign remains another important question.

No serious discussion about Zambian elections can ignore the importance of the Copperbelt.

Historically, whichever party performs strongly on the Copperbelt while maintaining support elsewhere significantly improves its chances nationally.

Recent opposition rallies in Kitwe have generated considerable public attention.

Combined with incidents such as the Chingola disturbances earlier in the campaign season, they suggest the province could once again become one of the country’s most competitive electoral battlegrounds.

Whether those crowds ultimately translate into votes will only become clear on election day.

Political campaigns are driven not only by numbers but by energy.

Successful campaigns often create a sense of momentum that motivates supporters to volunteer, donate and turn out in large numbers.

In both 2016 and 2021, the UPND benefited from this momentum while the PF struggled with declining enthusiasm among sections of its traditional support base.

Some observers believe the current campaign shows signs of a similar shift, with the Tonse Pamodzi Alliance generating increasing excitement among opposition supporters.

Others argue the UPND still enjoys overwhelming organisational advantages.

Both views may contain elements of truth.

Another factor that could influence voter behaviour is the lingering emotional impact surrounding the death and burial dispute involving former President Edgar Lungu.

Throughout the legal battles, Makebi Zulu emerged as one of the family’s most visible defenders.

For many PF supporters and sympathisers, that role strengthened his public profile beyond his legal credentials.

Whether this develops into a measurable sympathy vote remains uncertain, but political emotions often influence elections as much as policy debates.

Despite the opposition’s growing visibility, President Hichilema still enters the election with significant advantages.

He remains the incumbent, commands substantial state visibility, and continues to campaign on achievements including debt restructuring, expanded mining investment, increased electricity generation, free education and macroeconomic stability.

These remain important campaign assets.

At the same time, the Tonse Pamodzi Alliance has succeeded in making itself part of the national political conversation in a relatively short period.

That alone represents a notable political development.

The August election is therefore unlikely to be decided by campaign rhetoric alone. It will depend on organisation, turnout, regional voting patterns and each party’s ability to convert enthusiasm into ballots.

One lesson from Zambia’s democratic history remains clear: political momentum should never be dismissed too early, and no serious challenger should be underestimated.

The voters, as always, will have the final word.

THE EYE VIEWpublishes every week, providing informed analysis and opinion on politics, governance, business, sport and social affairs. The views expressed are those of the author and do not necessarily reflect the editorial position of Zambian Eye.

title: Samakayi quits UPND weeks after Bill 7 vote rebuke by Hichilema
summary: Former Mwinilunga Member of Parliament Newton Samakayi has resigned from the United Party for National Development, marking a significant setback for the ruling party in North-Western Province just weeks after President Hakainde Hichilema publicly singled him out over the controversial Bill 7 vote.
content:
Former Mwinilunga Member of Parliament Newton Samakayi has resigned from the United Party for National Development, marking a significant setback for the ruling party in North-Western Province just weeks after President Hakainde Hichilema publicly singled him out over the controversial Bill 7 vote.

In a resignation letter circulated recently, Samakayi confirmed his exit from the UPND with immediate effect. The announcement comes less than a month after President Hichilema named Samakayi as one of four UPND lawmakers who failed to support the Constitution Amendment Bill No. 7 during a highly charged parliamentary session.

Newton Samakayi served as Mwinilunga MP under the UPND ticket and was regarded as one of the party’s key voices in North-Western Province, a region that delivered overwhelming support to the UPND in the 2021 general elections.

Before entering Parliament, Samakayi built a profile as a grassroots mobilizer and party loyalist in Mwinilunga District, an area bordering the Democratic Republic of Congo where the UPND has historically competed fiercely with other parties for influence. In Parliament, he was known for focusing on rural development, border trade issues, health infrastructure and road connectivity for remote constituencies in the province.

While not a Cabinet Minister, Samakayi was part of the UPND backbench that the party relied on to maintain its legislative majority. Colleagues describe him as a calm but firm debater who often articulated the concerns of rural voters within the party caucus. His long association with the UPND predates the 2021 victory, having been active during the party’s years in opposition when North-Western was consolidating as a UPND stronghold.

*How important was he to the party?*

Samakayi’s importance to the UPND went beyond his single seat.

First, Mwinilunga is politically strategic. North-Western Province is one of the UPND’s traditional bases, and holding Mwinilunga was critical to the party’s narrative of broad national support beyond its Southern and Western Province heartlands. Losing an incumbent MP in that region weakens the party’s grassroots machinery ahead of the 2026 general elections.

Second, Samakayi was part of the UPND’s parliamentary numbers game. The ruling party holds a majority, but it is not large enough to absorb defections without consequence. With the Constitution Amendment Bill No. 7 requiring a two-thirds majority, every UPND vote counted. When President Hichilema publicly named Samakayi and three others for not supporting Bill 7, it exposed cracks within the caucus at a time when party discipline was being tested to its limit.

Third, Samakayi carried influence among local structures. As a sitting MP from the province, he was a link between the party’s national leadership and ward-level mobilizers in Mwinilunga. His resignation risks demoralizing UPND cadres in the district and could embolden opposition parties seeking to make inroads in North-Western ahead of 2026.

Political analyst Winstone Chibwe said Samakayi’s departure “is not just about one MP. It signals dissatisfaction within the UPND rank and file over how internal dissent is being handled.”

“Bill 7 became a loyalty test. When the President named those who did not vote for it, it put them in a difficult position with the base. For a party that prides itself on unity, losing a North-Western MP is a blow to both its numbers and its image,” Chibwe said.

Constitution Amendment Bill No. 7 dominated Zambia’s political debate in recent weeks. Proponents argued it would streamline governance, while critics warned it risked altering key democratic safeguards. The vote became a defining moment for the UPND caucus, with the party leadership pushing for unanimous support.

President Hichilema’s decision to publicly name Samakayi and three other UPND MPs who did not vote for the Bill was unusual and signaled zero tolerance for rebellion within the ranks. For Samakayi, being identified by the Head of State appears to have made his continued stay in the party untenable.

In his resignation statement, Samakayi did not give detailed reasons, but sources close to him say the public rebuke and subsequent pressure from party structures influenced his decision.

*What next for UPND and Mwinilunga?*

The UPND now faces the task of holding Mwinilunga in a possible by-election, should Samakayi’s resignation trigger a vacancy in Parliament. The seat will be a test of whether the party can retain support in North-Western without an incumbent who had built personal goodwill in the constituency.

For Samakayi, his political future is unclear. He has not announced which party, if any, he will join next. Some analysts speculate he could either go independent or be courted by opposition groupings seeking to capitalize on discontent within the UPND.

The UPND has not yet issued an official response to the resignation. Party Secretary General Batuke Imenda is expected to comment in the coming days.

Samakayi’s exit adds to a growing list of internal challenges for the ruling party as it navigates constitutional reforms, economic pressures and preparations for the August 13, 2026 general election. Whether his departure remains an isolated case or the start of a wider trend will likely determine how much damage the UPND sustains in the months ahead.

title: Xenophobic tendencies will kill our South African dream
summary: By Prof. Bismark Tyobeka, principal and vice-chancellor of the North-West University (NWU).
content:
By Prof. Bismark Tyobeka, principal and vice-chancellor of the North-West University (NWU).

Twice I was there when South Africa was the centre of the universe. The last decade of the 20th century heralded the arrival of a nation poised to lead through moral example, as we pledged never again to repeat our tainted history. Today, many citizens threaten violence against foreigners, and once again we face becoming one of the world’s pariahs.

On 11 February 1990, I was glued to the television in my sister’s home in the township of Selosesha in Thaba Nchu, where I had just started Standard 8 (Grade 10) at Moroka High School. I had taken a weekend off from the school hostel and, together with a global audience, watched as Nelson Mandela walked free from Victor Verster Prison. Four years later, on 27 April 1994, I was in my village of Nonceba, where I cast my vote at Nonceba Primary School. South Africa opened its arms to all who live in it, and, in turn, the world opened its arms to us.

Now, 32 years and two months later, as principal and vice-chancellor of the North-West University (NWU), I am aghast at the danger of large-scale violence and disruptions aimed at illegal immigrants.

First, I want us to be honest. There is no place for illegal immigration. We have limited resources, and it is our obligation to look after and safeguard our citizens. After the recent spate of xenophobic attacks and the promise of more to come, South Africans are once again being asked to choose between two false extremes. Either they must tolerate xenophobia, or they must ignore illegal immigration. Collectively, we should choose neither. We have to acknowledge that when governments fail to manage immigration properly, ordinary migrants often become scapegoats for failures that belong to the state. This is just as great an injustice as immigrants crossing our borders illegally. Violence, however, is never the answer.

I want to bring the argument closer to home. My home. The North-West University. The worduniversitystems from the Latinuniversitas, which translates as “a community united as a whole”. At the NWU, we have lecturers, researchers and students from across our continent because we know that major scientific breakthroughs cross borders, that we have as much to teach others as we have to learn from them, and that ideas have passports far more powerful than those carried by people.

Students become better graduates because they encounter people who think differently, and a university that isolates itself quickly becomes intellectually poorer.

In the public’s eye, the distinction between lawful migrants, refugees, international students and skilled professionals, and those who deliberately circumvent immigration law, is disappearing. When that happens, we arrive at the toxic situation we face today.

As public universities, I believe it is our duty to demonstrate that openness and order are not opposing ideals, but complementary ones. We thrive because we combine openness to people and ideas with accountability, merit and respect for the rule of law. International students do not simply arrive; they obtain the necessary visas. International academics are appointed through rigorous processes and are expected to comply with the same regulations and standards as everyone else. Openness works precisely because it is underpinned by clear rules that are applied fairly and consistently.

A recent article in the respected British publicationThe Economiststates that there is ample evidence to challenge many common assumptions about migration. Foreign-born residents make up only about 5% of South Africa’s population, and research by the World Bank and the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development suggests that migrants are often net job creators who establish businesses and stimulate economic activity. Studies have also found that foreign nationals are, on average, less likely to commit crime than South African citizens. These are hard truths, but are they widely known?

The worduniversitasreminds us that humanity advances not by turning away from one another, but by learning together. Yet communities also endure because they respect rules. South Africa does not have to choose between openness and order. We need both. We must reject xenophobia without reservation, enforce immigration law without apology, and continue building universities that welcome ideas from every corner of the world while remaining firmly rooted in the rule of law.

The colours of our Rainbow Nation are fading. The dreams of our founders are being deferred. The blood spilled in the pursuit of freedom has been forgotten, and their beliefs are being neglected and misused. More than 11 750 days after I cast my first democratic vote, the legacy of reconciliation over revenge and hope over hatred remains ours to protect. Let us take up a new canvas and use this opportunity to paint it brightly once again, so that the world may see that South Africa is more than a place on a map: it is an idea to aspire to.

title: Spax Mining convicted of tax evasion
summary: The Zambia Revenue Authority (ZRA) in its desire to crack down on tax evasion in the country, instituted criminal proceedings against Spax Mining Limited on the Copperbelt on charges of furnishing false statements and returns contrary to sections 43 of the Value Added Tax Act Chapter 331 of the l...
content:
The Zambia Revenue Authority (ZRA) in its desire to crack down on tax evasion in the country, instituted criminal proceedings against Spax Mining Limited on the Copperbelt on charges of furnishing false statements and returns contrary to sections 43 of the Value Added Tax Act Chapter 331 of the laws of Zambia.

Facts of the case are that on dates unknown but between the 1st January 2022 and 31st August 2023, jointly and whilst acting together with others unknown, in purported compliance with the law, did provide information to the Zambia Revenue Authority purporting to show that Spax Mining Limited was supplied with taxable supplies namely ores and copper ores on various tax invoice numbers by Wise Focus Mining Limited and Zamgem International Limited for the period June 2022 to August, 2023 which was false in material particular.

This fraudulent action by the taxpayer, resulted in loss of Government revenue in Value Added Tax amounting to K4,490,523.68.

When the case came up for pre-trial conference on 19th November 2024, the accused person informed the court that they wanted to invoke the provisions of the Plea Negotiations Act Number 20 of 2010.

The State did not object to the application provided the accused persons paid in full the evaded Value Added Tax amount of K4,490,523.68 and refund the cost incurred by the State in prosecuting the case in the sum of K100,000.00.

The accused person paid the said amounts to the Zambia Revenue Authority forthwith. Upon the convict’s own admission of guilty, the Economic and Financial Crimes Court convicted and sentenced the convict to pay a fine of K13,500.00 forthwith in default to serve Six (6) months simple imprisonment.

Cases of Value Added Tax fraud and tax evasion are on the rise on the Copperbelt and the country at large, therefore ZRA has intensified its monitoring mechanisms to detect tax fraud.

All taxpayers found wanting will be prosecuted in accordance with the law.

title: INTRODUCING: The Eye View – A Weekly Commentary. Fresh Perspectives. National Conversations.
summary: Every week, events shape conversations across our communities — in politics, sport, religion, business and everyday social life. Some stories raise difficult questions. Others inspire hope. Many deserve deeper thought beyond breaking news and social media reactions.
content:
Every week, events shape conversations across our communities — in politics, sport, religion, business and everyday social life. Some stories raise difficult questions. Others inspire hope. Many deserve deeper thought beyond breaking news and social media reactions.

That is why we are introducingTHE EYE VIEW.

The Eye Viewis Zambian Eye’s new weekly commentary and analysis column — a dedicated space for reflection, ideas and informed opinion on the issues shaping Zambia and the world around us.

Published every week and written by selected contributors, the column will go beyond reporting events to exploringwhat they mean, why they matter, and where they may lead us.

From election campaigns and governance debates…To football rivalries and sporting moments…From matters of faith and values…To culture, society and the changing lives of ordinary citizens…

The Eye Viewwill bring perspectives designed to challenge assumptions, stimulate discussion and encourage readers to think differently.

The column will not seek to tell readers what to believe.

Instead, it will ask questions, examine trends, present viewpoints and open conversations that continue long after the week ends.

Some articles may agree with popular opinion. Others may challenge it.

But all contributions will share one objective:

To help readers see beyond the headline.

Because every story has another angle.

And every week…The Eye will have a view.

New edition every week — only on Zambian Eye.

title: Mundubile, Makebi link Lungu’s burial votes
summary: Tonse PF-Pamodzi Alliance Presidential candidate Brian Mundubile has said he and his running mate Makebi Zulu will only proceed with the burial of former President Edgar Lungu if they win the August 13 elections.
content:
Tonse PF-Pamodzi Alliance Presidential candidate Brian Mundubile has said he and his running mate Makebi Zulu will only proceed with the burial of former President Edgar Lungu if they win the August 13 elections.

Speaking at a rally in Chinsali, Muchinga Province this afternoon, Mundubile told supporters that Lungu deserves to be laid to rest with dignity and respect.

“What you must know is that our father Edgar Lungu has not yet been buried. What Makebi Zulu and I have agreed on is that when you vote for us and we win, we will honour late President Edgar Lungu and bury him with respect.

“Our father needs to be buried with respect,” Mundubile said.

Former Zambian President Lungu died on June 5, 2025, in Pretoria, South Africa, while receiving medical treatment.

His family confirmed the death, saying he had been ill for some time.

Lungu led Zambia from 2015 to 2021 after taking over following President Michael Sata’s death, and then winning the 2016 election.

He lost to Hakainde Hichilema in the 2021 polls and left office.

The circumstances around his burial immediately became a political and legal dispute between the Lungu family, the Patriotic Front, and the United Party for National Development government.

After Lungu’s death, the family said they wanted a private burial in South Africa, arguing that there was no trust between them and the state.

They cited unresolved tensions dating back to 2021, when the new government stripped Lungu of some retirement benefits, accused him of interfering in politics, and opened investigations linked to his time in office.

The state, for its part, said a former head of state was entitled to an official state funeral in Zambia, with full military honours and burial at Embassy Park in Lusaka, the site reserved for presidents.

The government obtained a court interdict to stop the burial in South Africa.

The family countered that an official funeral would be used for political purposes and that Lungu had expressed a wish to be buried privately. The standoff left Lungu’s body in a Pretoria mortuary for weeks, with no burial taking place.

The matter was heard in the Pretoria High Court in South Africa in July 2025. The court dismissed the Zambian government’s application to have Lungu’s body returned to Zambia for a state funeral.

Judge President Dunstan Mlambo ruled that the South African court had no jurisdiction to order the repatriation of a body against the wishes of the next of kin, and that the decision on burial arrangements rested with the family under South African law.

The judge also said the dispute was essentially political and not one that could be resolved by a foreign court imposing a state ceremony.

The ruling effectively meant the Zambian state could not compel a state burial. Following the judgment, the family maintained that burial would only proceed on terms they considered acceptable. As of mid-2026, Lungu had not been buried.

The issue has remained central to political debate ahead of the August 13, 2026 general election, with opposition leaders, including Tonse PF-Pamodzi candidate Brian Mundubile, saying they would ensure Lungu is buried with respect if they win power.

The case highlighted the intersection of customary family authority, constitutional provisions for former presidents, and the deep political divisions that persisted after Lungu left office.

title: Why Online Fashion Returns Are Out of Control
summary: Online fashion returns hit 30-40% in some categories. The math is brutal for retailers and shoppers. Here’s what virtual try-on actually changes.
content:
Online fashion returns hit 30-40% in some categories. The math is brutal for retailers and shoppers. Here’s what virtual try-on actually changes.

The return rate problem in online fashion has been a slow-rolling disaster for fifteen years. Brick-and-mortar fashion sees roughly an 8 to 10 percent return rate. Online fashion sees 25 to 30 percent on average, with some categories (occasion wear, denim, premium outerwear) hitting 40 to 50 percent. ASOS reportedly absorbs return rates north of 30 percent on its dress category. Zalando openly discusses returns as one of its largest operational costs.

Every return is a small disaster: a customer who didn’t get what they wanted, an item that may not be resellable, transportation emissions, processing labor, sometimes destruction of the unit. Retailers respond with “free returns” policies that paper over the underlying problem and a few have started charging for them. Neither approach fixes the root cause: shoppers can’t tell whether an item will look right on them before buying.

Look at the actual reasons shoppers give for returns:

The fit was wrong. The single largest category, by a wide margin. The dress didn’t drape right, the trousers were too tight, the jacket sat oddly on the shoulders.

The color was different in person. Photography and screens both lie. A color that reads dusty pink on the product page can arrive bright coral.

The proportions read differently on a real body than on the model. A long blazer that looks structured on a 5’10” model can read shapeless on a 5’4″ shopper.

The fabric felt wrong. Cheap synthetic when the photos suggested wool, scratchy when the photos suggested soft.

It just looked different than I expected. The catchall reason, often a combination of the above.

The first four are partially addressable. The fifth is where the entire category needs new tools.

In a physical store, the fitting room solves a specific problem: it lets you see yourself in the garment. You confirm proportions, you confirm color against your skin, you confirm whether the garment “reads right.” You can’t confirm fabric softness from a distance, but you can confirm the visual.

The fitting room doesn’t make every garment look great. It just gives you the information to choose. Online shoppers have been making purchase decisions without that information for fifteen years.

A good AI try-on tool reproduces the fitting room mirror digitally. You provide a face photo and a body photo. The tool takes a product image from any retailer and renders you in the actual garment, at the right scale, with your face, your build, your skin tone. The rendering shows what the photographer’s model rendering can’t show: how the item looks on you specifically. If you want tostop returning what you buy online, this is the workflow that actually moves the number.

The proportions question. The dress that hits mid-thigh on a 5’10” model hits closer to the knee on a 5’4″ shopper. The rendering shows this honestly, before the shopper clicks buy.

The color-against-skin question. The dusty pink that looks great on a fair model may read sallow against a warm skin tone. The rendering shows this honestly.

One thing it doesn’t fix: actual fit. The render shows what fit would look like, not whether the size 8 trousers will actually fit a particular waist. Brand-specific size variation, fabric behavior, and individual measurements still matter.

Even partial improvement is meaningful. If a retailer’s category return rate is 30 percent and try-on cuts the visual-fit subset by half, the overall return rate drops to 20-22 percent. On a category doing $50 million in revenue, that’s $4 million in saved logistics costs, plus the items that stay sold rather than returning to inventory.

For shoppers, the math is similar but personal. The average online fashion shopper returns one of every three to four orders. Going from three returns per year to one or zero returns per year is real time savings, real money saved on partial shipping fees where charged, real reduction in cart-and-return cycle drag.

Three patterns are visible now:

Some retailers have integrated try-on at checkout. Farfetch, Wolford, and several Nordstrom-tier sites use Aiuta or Veesual under the hood. The shopper sees a “try on virtually” button next to the size dropdown and gets a render in seconds.

Cross-retailer try-on apps have emerged for shoppers who buy across many stores. The Styl10 wedge is “paste any retailer URL.” The shopper uses one app across Nordstrom, Gap, Zara, Vuori. This is the closest analog to a personal stylist with a fitting room.

Native phone-app try-on is still uneven. Some retailer apps have polished implementations, others are clunky or unavailable. The mobile experience is still maturing.

The path of least friction for most shoppers is to use a cross-retailer try-on for items where you’re uncertain, and skip it for items you’ve bought before and know fit you well.

A few honest weaknesses to plan around:

Fabric behavior. Render shows visual, not feel. A linen that looks crisp can be scratchy in person.

Items that depend on movement. Drape, swing, how a fabric moves. Static renders can’t show this.

Items at the edge of your typical sizing. If you’re between sizes, the render shows one size; the other might fit better. Reading size reviews still matters.

Items in unusual lighting. Sequins, metallics, and high-shine fabrics can render differently than they look in real photography.

For these specific failure modes, read product descriptions, check sizing reviews, and use return policies as the backstop. For everything else, virtual try-on closes most of the gap between “looks good in the photo” and “looks good on me.”

The shift to virtual try-on as the default isn’t a question of whether. It’s a question of when. The economics work for retailers, the experience works for shoppers, the technology is ready. The shopper who sets up a try-on tool now will compound the benefit across every shopping decision they make for the next several years. The retailer that doesn’t offer try-on (or doesn’t make it easy to find) is going to keep eating returns. The fitting room moved online. The shoppers who notice will return less.

title: I used to block people who called me ugly but not anymore – Natasha BlackChild
summary: EVEN with the powerful slogan “black is beautiful,” Natasha Mwaba, a social media influencer has fallen victim to the scourge of cyber bullying due to her dark complexion, as the ugly specter of Colourism continues to rear its head in the digital realm.
content:
EVEN with the powerful slogan “black is beautiful,” Natasha Mwaba, a social media influencer has fallen victim to the scourge of cyber bullying due to her dark complexion, as the ugly specter of Colourism continues to rear its head in the digital realm.

The internet of late has increasingly become a breeding ground for vicious attacks on appearance, with most online users wielding the power of public opinion as a weapon of humiliation.

Despite legislations aimed at curbing cybercrimes, the toxic culture of cyberbullying continues to thrive, with targets frequently subjected to sarcastic or bitter attacks on everything from their physical features to their skin color.

Defying the attack of cyberbullying aimed at undermining her self-esteem, Natasha has refused to buckle under the pressure, standing firm in the conviction that her beauty cannot be dimmed by cruel online remarks.

Natasha shared with #Kalemba her coping strategy for navigating the unpredictable waters of social media bullying, emphasising the importance of emotional resilience and the power of positive self-talk.

She recounted that many are the times she used to cry and block people who called her ugly but not anymore, as she has learned to focus on self- acceptance and positive self-talk instead of giving bullies the power to define her.

“Social media bullying can be incredibly distressing, but I have learned to handle it by ignoring the hateful comments and disengaging from the bullies. Instead of wasting energy crying and blocking people, I choose to focus on positive self-affirmations and the support of my friends. By centering myself on positivity, I’m able to rise above the negativity and find strength in my own sense of self-worth.”

She asserted that she remains black, beautiful and no amount of hateful comments or attacks can shake her self confidence and make her doubt her worth.

“I have focused on my strengths and passions, I surround myself with positive influencers and practice self-care and self-compassion because I believe that online opinions don’t define my worth,” she said.

“We need to promote a culture of kindness and respect so we can create a more supportive online space for everyone.”

Addressing her fellow social media users, particularly those who have been subjected to bullying and aspiring influencers alike, Natasha implored them to harness the power of their platforms for the greater good.

“Let us use our voices and our influence to build each other up, not tear each other down. If you are struggling with bullying or self-doubt, remember that you are not alone, and you have the power to rise above the negativity,” she added.

“To those who are just starting their journey as influencers, let us be role models for kindness and positivity.”

Despite being targeted for her dark complexion with hurtful insults hurled her way, Natasha remains resilient, defiantly embracing her beauty and refusing to let the bullies dictate her sense of self-worth.

title: Correctional Service giving Kambwili proper medical attention – HRC
summary: THE Human Rights Commission (HRC) has assured the public that former cabinet minister Chishimba Kambwili is receiving adequate medical attention while in custody at Lusaka Central Correctional Service Facility.
content:
THE Human Rights Commission (HRC) has assured the public that former cabinet minister Chishimba Kambwili is receiving adequate medical attention while in custody at Lusaka Central Correctional Service Facility.

This follows concerns raised in sections of the media, opposition Patriotic Front (PF) and his family that Kambwili’s health was deteriorating and not being adequately managed in prison.

In a statement, HRC spokesperson Mweelwa Muleya revealed that the Commission conducted an inspection visit to Lusaka Central Correctional Service on August 13 to independently verify Kambwili’s health status.

According to the Commission’s findings, Kambwili was transferred from Kasama Central Correctional Facility to Lusaka on August 5, 2025 to attend court sessions.

Upon arrival, he was admitted to the facility’s clinic to cater for his health needs, including being given access to an elevated bed.

Muleya explained that the Commission held separate interactions with Kambwili, Correctional Service officers and medical staff in order to establish his health situation and to determine if there was a need for remedial action.

“The public may wish to know that the finding of the Commission was that the Zambia Correctional Service was facilitating medical access to Dr Kambwili and so far, the Commission was not privy to any report indicating that his health needs were beyond the capacity of the medical personnel that were attending to him,” said Muleya.

The HRC further clarified that its visit to Kambwili took place even before PF acting president Given Lubinda officially lodged a complaint on August 15, regarding his alleged deteriorating health.

Muleya stated that the Commission is bound by law to respect the right to privacy of every person, including the protection of medical information that comes to its attention during investigations.

He added that the Commission would continue to monitor the situation closely but would only intervene further if advised by qualified medical personnel that the former minister’s condition required attention beyond what the Correctional Service could provide.

Kambwili is currently serving a five-month prison sentence after his conviction on November 2023 for hate speech.

The court found him guilty of making tribal remarks against the people of Southern Province, contrary to Section 70 (1) of the Penal Code, which prohibits expressions of hatred or ridicule based on tribe or place of origin.

His appeal was dismissed by the High Court in Kasama on July 14, 2025 and he decided not to appeal but serve the sentence.

title: End expectation that gospel music must be free – Deborah Mambo
summary: FOR far too long, there has been a misguided perception that gospel music should be provided free of charge, while secular music is marketed and sold with no such expectations.
content:
FOR far too long, there has been a misguided perception that gospel music should be provided free of charge, while secular music is marketed and sold with no such expectations.

This unfair double standard is believed to be undermining the value of gospel music and negatively impacting the livelihoods of those who devote their time and talent to creating it.

Gospel artiste Deborah Mambo, opened up about the various challenges that most gospel musicians face in the industry.

According to Mambo, many people seem to believe that gospel music should be freely accessible without any cost, while failing to recognise the significant investment of time, energy and creativity that goes into producing high-quality music.

Mambo highlighted the immense pressure gospel musicians experience while trying to maintain their religious mission and earn enough to cover their expenses.

“In Zambia, gospel artistes find themselves in a difficult predicament. We are committed to sharing our faith through our music, but this often comes at the expense of our financial well-being,” she said.

“The high costs associated with recording, marketing, and performing can be challenging to cover, leaving many gospel musicians struggling to break even.”

She emphasised the need for public support and recognition to ensure the growth and sustainability of the industry.

“Despite the significant effort put in by gospel musicians, the prevailing public attitude remains that our music should be free. This attitude hinders the development and expansion of our industry,” the gospel artiste shared.

She said in order to see the gospel music industry thrive in Zambia, there is need for public support and understanding that public assistance means investing in the future of the nation’s music scene.

Meanwhile, Mambo highlighted the upward trajectory of Zambian gospel music, noting that it is slowly gaining recognition beyond borders.

She attributed this progress to improved production standards and higher quality recordings, emphasising that the industry has made significant strides in recent years.

With greater public understanding and support, the gospel music scene in the country has the potential to flourish, creating a richer, more diverse music landscape for all Zambians to enjoy.

title: GSDI calls for disability inclusion as a right, not charity
summary: GOOD Shepherds Disability Initiative (GSDI) has called on communities, churches, government institutions, and civil society to embrace disability inclusion as a right, not charity.
content:
GOOD Shepherds Disability Initiative (GSDI) has called on communities, churches, government institutions, and civil society to embrace disability inclusion as a right, not charity.

The local non-profit, based in Kabompo said persons with disabilities in Kabompo District deserve full participation in education, health, livelihoods and national development.

“We are championing the rights of persons with disabilities. Inclusion is not optional, it is a constitutional right,” said GSDI chairperson Jonathan Sakuwunda.

GSDI works closely with the Zambia Agency for Persons with Disabilities (ZAPD) to register persons with disabilities, raise awareness about their rights and provide access to services.

Parents, caregivers and volunteers are also trained to support persons with disabilities in their communities.

The NGO has backed the recent assenting of Bill 7 into law, which aimed at strengthening representation, equality and participation of persons with disabilities in national governance.

Sakuwunda said membership to GSDI is open to all, including persons with disabilities, parents, supporters and volunteers who share the organization’s mission of building an inclusive Zambia.

“Together, we can ensure that no one is left behind. Every ability counts,” the chairperson added.

title: K2.8 million births education, health, trading infrastructures in Chilanga under CDF
summary: RESIDENTS of New Farms Ward and Chinyanja Ward in Chilanga Constituency have welcomed major development projects funded under the Constituency Development Fund (CDF), with more than K2.8 million invested in health, education and trading infrastructure.
content:
RESIDENTS of New Farms Ward and Chinyanja Ward in Chilanga Constituency have welcomed major development projects funded under the Constituency Development Fund (CDF), with more than K2.8 million invested in health, education and trading infrastructure.

In New Farms Ward, a maternity wing annex at HST Hospice was constructed at a cost of K695,682.57, bringing much-needed maternity services closer to expectant mothers who previously had to travel long distances.

Meanwhile, in Chinyanja Ward, several projects were commissioned: a market shelter at Chinyanja Market built at K1,613,062.04 together with an ablution block and a water tank to improve sanitation for traders, and electrification of Westwood Primary School at K582,000, which will improve learning conditions and allow pupils to do computer practicals on-site.

Speaking during the commissioning ceremony, Chilanga Member of Parliament Sipho Hlazo said the projects demonstrate government’s commitment to taking development closer to communities.

“It is with great pride and gratitude that I stand before you today for the official handover and commissioning of three important projects financed under the Constituency Development Fund: the maternity wing annex at HST Hospice in New Farms Ward, the electrification of Westwood Primary School, and the market shelter at Chinyanja Market,” Hlazo said.

Hlazo explained that the maternity annex was constructed to ensure mothers access safe delivery services without travelling long distances.

“The President’s vision is for every mother in Zambia to deliver her child in a clean, safe and dignified environment. This maternity annex therefore demonstrates our commitment to ensuring that no woman is exposed to unnecessary risks of travelling long distances to access safe maternity services,” he said.

Chilanga Council chairperson Champion Tembo said the projects show how the CDF programme is transforming communities.

“This event is not just about handing over a building. It is about celebrating a government programme that is transforming communities and improving the lives of our people,” Tembo said.

Residents of New Farms Ward expressed gratitude for the new maternity facility.

Sister-in-charge Linet Kasongo said the facility would ease the burden on expectant mothers who previously travelled far.

“We are grateful to government for easing the distances mothers used to cover and for trusting us to continue caring for patients through this new maternity ward,” Kasongo said.

New Farms Ward resident Mercy Chikanko said women often faced serious challenges when labour started because of poor transport.

“Transport here is a challenge. You can be in labour and start looking for transport while the place you are going to is very far. Now we are grateful for what they have done for us by bringing the maternity ward closer to our homes,” she said.

At Westwood Primary School in Chinyanja Ward, headteacher Georgina Mumba said the school has struggled with electricity challenges since it was established in 2010.

“Since 2010, we have faced challenges especially during examinations when printing was needed. Pupils learning computer studies had to be transported to other facilities, but now with electricity at the school the situation will improve and the quality of education will also improve,” Mumba said.

She also thanked government for the free education policy and the school feeding programme, which has helped improve attendance.

“We thank the President for the feeding programme and also free education. It has brought a lot of development in this school because absenteeism has reduced,” she said.

Meanwhile, pupil representative Lugowe Mukubesa said pupils were grateful that they will now be able to conduct computer practical lessons on-site.

“We are humbled by the proper use of CDF. Computer practicals will not be going somewhere else but we will do them within the school. We will guide these things properly because you have entrusted us with them,” Mukubesa said.

During the same event, the area Member of Parliament also donated 180 desks to Westwood Primary School to improve the learning environment.

Traders at Chinyanja Market also welcomed the newly commissioned facilities.

Trader Betty Kasonkwe said business had been difficult because traders previously operated from their homes without proper market facilities.

“Trading here has not been easy because we used to conduct our business from home and we never had a proper market before,” she said.

Officials urged residents of both wards to take ownership of the newly constructed infrastructure and safeguard it so that it continues serving the community for generations to come.

title: ‘DON’T VOTE FOR MWEETWA’
summary: CORNELIUS Mweetwa must be taught a bitter lesson by the electorate by not voting for him in the August polls for trying to make fools of the people through a gimmick designed to portray him as most favoured candidate, the Young African Leaders Initiative (YALI) has said.
content:
CORNELIUS Mweetwa must be taught a bitter lesson by the electorate by not voting for him in the August polls for trying to make fools of the people through a gimmick designed to portray him as most favoured candidate, the Young African Leaders Initiative (YALI) has said.

Inside sources revealed that Mr Mweetwa sponsored a group of youths to demand that he rescinds his decision not to re-contest the Choma Central parliamentary seat in the August 12 elections to create a picture that he was the most wanted candidate.

YALI president Andrew Ntewewe however said Mr Mweetwa was undermining the calibre of electorates in Choma by thinking they could be hoodwinked by simple gimmicks.

Mr. Ntewewe said it was an insult for Mr Mweetwa to insinuate that he was the only worthy candidate to represent the people of Choma in parliament.

He called on Choma residents to teach Mr Mweetwa a bitter lesson by not voting for him in the upcoming elections. Controversy has emerged after Mr Mweetwa’s gimmick to portray himself as the most popular candidate was exposed.

On Sunday, Mr Mweetwa announced that he would not re-contest the Choma seat which he had held for the past two decades on the pretext that he wanted to concentrate on taking UPND president Hakainde Hichilema to State House.

But speaking at a media briefing on Monday, Mr Mweetwa rescinded his decision, claiming that after the announcement on Sunday, he had received a lot of criticism and misinformation, alleging that he had been bought by the ruling Patriotic Front.
